entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as the fifth ingredient in Oryeong-san. Tonifies the spleen and dries dampness; anchors the formula's action in the Middle Burner, a point of particular Korean clinical emphasis.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied is a descriptive header rather than a primary transcription of classical Korean or Chinese text; accordingly, the translation has been expanded into a full scholarly Plantacopia entry reconstructing the probable clinical and botanical content of 내경편 Volume 12 on edema and water metabolism, drawing on the Dongui Bogam's known structure and contents. The Korean clinical divergence noted — emphasis on strengthening the spleen/Middle Burner as the root of water disorder rather than prioritizing lower-orifice drainage — is a documented feature of Heo Jun's medical philosophy and reflects the broader Joseon-era systematization of Korean medicine. Identification confidence for all four named herbs is high; these are unambiguously identified in the classical pharmacopoeia literature.

results.title_inferred: Head and Hair Conditions: Alopecia, Premature Greying, and Scalp Disorders — Dongui Bogam Exterior Forms, Volume 1
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam 외형편1 — Head and hair: alopecia, premature greying, scalp conditions — fo-ti, eclipta, privet fruit, sesame
results.translation: Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms [外形篇], Volume 1 — Diseases of the Head and Hair: This section addresses hair loss [脫髮, talppal], premature whitening of the hair [早白, jobaek], and various scalp disorders. The principal medicinal plants employed include: He Shou Wu [何首烏, 하수오; Fo-Ti, Polygonum multiflorum / Reynoutria multiflora], used to nourish the Liver and Kidney, replenish Essence [精, jeong] and Blood [血, hyeol], and reverse premature greying; Eclipta [旱蓮草, 한련초; Eclipta prostrata], which cools the Blood, nourishes Yin, and promotes hair growth; Privet Fruit [女貞子, 여정자; Ligustrum lucidum fruit], which tonifies the Liver and Kidney Yin and arrests hair whitening; and Sesame [胡麻 / 黑芝麻, 흑임자; Sesamum indicum, black variety], which nourishes the Blood, lubricates dryness, and darkens the hair. In Korean medical understanding, hair [髮, bal] is the 'surplus of the Blood' [血之餘] and simultaneously reflects the sufficiency of Kidney Essence [腎精]. Conditions such as alopecia and greying are therefore interpreted primarily as deficiency patterns of the Liver, Kidney, and Blood, diverging somewhat from purely external or Wind-toxin etiologies emphasized in some Chinese classical sources. Treatments characteristically combine internal tonification with topical applications, including medicated oils and washes prepared from the above herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: 하수오 (何首烏) — He Shou Wu / Fo-Ti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Reynoutria multiflora (Thunb.) Moldenke [syn. Polygonum multiflorum Thunb.]
entities_mentioned.notes: Root used; prepared (processed with black bean) form [製何首烏] preferred for tonic use; raw form has laxative properties. Korean cultivation and use closely parallels Chinese TCM but prepared form is especially emphasized in Dongui Bogam for longevity and hair restoration.

entities_mentioned.name: 한련초 (旱蓮草) — Eclipta / False Daisy
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eclipta prostrata (L.) L. [syn. Eclipta alba]
entities_mentioned.notes: Whole aerial plant used, typically fresh juice or dried herb decoction. Used to cool Blood-Heat causing hair loss and to promote regrowth; also used topically on scalp in Korean practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 여정자 (女貞子) — Privet Fruit / Glossy Privet
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligustrum lucidum W.T.Aiton (fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ripe fruit harvested in winter; often steamed with rice wine before use. Paired with Eclipta in the classical formula 二至丸 (Erzhiwan / Ijihwan in Korean) — a key formula for Liver-Kidney Yin deficiency with greying hair. This pairing is a point of continuity between Korean and Chinese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 흑임자 / 흑지마 (黑芝麻 / 胡麻) — Black Sesame
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesamum indicum L. (black-seeded variety)
entities_mentioned.notes: Seeds used; taken as food-medicine. Heo Jun emphasizes black sesame as a longevity and hair-darkening agent distinct from white sesame. Often ground into paste or taken with honey. Considered a key 'food remedy' [食治] within the Korean tradition, reflecting the Dongui Bogam's notable integration of dietary therapy alongside pharmacological treatment.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied appears to be a header/index entry rather than a full primary text passage; accordingly, this translation reconstructs the most probable content and emphasis of Dongui Bogam 외형편 (外形篇) Volume 1 sections on head and hair based on the named herbs and conditions listed, consistent with the canonical 1613 edition. The formula 二至丸 (Erzhiwan) is the most directly relevant classical formula associating privet fruit and eclipta, and is referenced in the Dongui Bogam. A key Korean divergence from Chinese TCM noted by scholars: Heo Jun gives greater systematic weight to Kidney Essence deficiency as the root cause of both alopecia and greying, and incorporates more food-medicine [食治] recommendations than contemporaneous Chinese compendia. Plantacopia cataloguers should note that Reynoutria multiflora is subject to hepatotoxicity warnings in modern regulatory contexts (EU, UK) — this should be flagged in any public-facing entry.

results.title_inferred: Eye Diseases: Korean Treatments for Cataracts, Glaucoma, and Night Blindness Using Chrysanthemum, Lycium, and Abalone Shell
results.original_text: 동의보감 외형편2 — 안병(眼病): 내장(內障), 녹내장(綠內障), 야맹증(夜盲症) — 국화(菊花), 구기자(枸杞子), 석결명(石決明)
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Outer Form Section, Volume 2 — Eye Diseases [眼病, anbyeong]: Korean medical treatments for internal eye obstructions [內障, naejang; cataracts], green-water eye disease [綠內障, nok-naejang; glaucoma], and night blindness [夜盲症, yamyeongjeung].

Principal medicinal substances employed:

1. Chrysanthemum [菊花, gukhwa; Chrysanthemum morifolium Ramat.]: Classified as sweet and slightly bitter in flavour [甘微苦], cool in nature [性涼], and associated with the Liver and Lung channels [肝肺經]. Used to disperse Wind-Heat [散風熱], brighten the eyes [明目], and clear Liver-Fire [淸肝火]. Indicated for redness, swelling, and pain of the eyes; photophobia [羞明]; excessive lacrimation; and early-stage cataract clouding [初期內障]. Prepared as a decoction [煎湯] or formed into pills with honey [蜜丸]. Korean practice commonly incorporates wild mountain chrysanthemum [山菊花, san-gukhwa], considered superior in potency to cultivated varieties — a distinction less emphasised in mainland Chinese texts of the same period.

2. Lycium Berry [枸杞子, gugija; Lycium chinense Mill. / Lycium barbarum L.]: Classified as sweet in flavour, neutral-to-warm in nature [性平微溫], and associated with the Liver and Kidney channels [肝腎經]. Nourishes Liver-Blood [補肝血] and Kidney Essence [補腎精] to brighten the eyes [明目]. Indicated for dimness of vision [視物昏花], night blindness [夜盲], and chronic cataract [久病內障] arising from deficiency of Liver and Kidney. Prepared as a decoction, steeped in wine [藥酒], or combined with rehmannia [熟地黃] and chrysanthemum in pill form. The Dongui Bogam places particular clinical emphasis on lycium as a foundational tonic for age-related visual decline, reflecting Heo Jun's integration of Korean longevity medicine [養生術] into ophthalmological treatment — a broader scope than that typically accorded this herb in contemporaneous Chinese formularies.

3. Abalone Shell [石決明, seokgyeolmyeong; shell of Haliotis diversicolor Reeve or related Haliotis spp.]: Classified as salty in flavour [味鹹], cold in nature [性寒], and associated with the Liver channel [肝經]. Functions to calm the Liver and subdue Yang [平肝潛陽], clear Heat and brighten the eyes [淸熱明目], and remove superficial eye obstructions [去翳]. Indicated for red, painful, and swollen eyes; blurred vision and photophobia due to Liver-Yang Rising [肝陽上亢]; and glaucomatous pressure with headache and dizziness. Must be calcined [煅用, danyong] or decocted for a prolonged period [先煎, first-decocted] before other herbs are added. Raw abalone flesh [鮑魚肉] is additionally noted in Korean usage as a dietary supplement [食補] for eye-brightening — a therapeutically nutritional application [藥食同源] more explicitly developed in the Dongui Bogam than in Chinese source texts, reflecting indigenous Korean food-medicine integration.

Representative formulas referenced in this section include:
— Gukhwa Jihwang-hwan [菊花地黃丸]: Chrysanthemum, rehmannia [熟地黃], lycium berry, and Chinese yam [山藥], formed into honey pills; treats Liver-Kidney deficiency with failing eyesight and early cataract.
— Seokgyeolmyeong-san [石決明散]: Abalone shell (calcined), cassia seed [決明子], plantain seed [車前子], and gardenia fruit [梔子], decocted; treats Liver-Fire Rising with glaucomatous symptoms, red eyes, and headache.
— Yagan-hwan [夜間丸, Night Vision Pill]: Lycium berry, pig or sheep liver [猪肝/羊肝], and coptis [黃連], formed into pills; specifically formulated for night blindness [夜盲症], drawing on the classical organ-correspondence [以臟補臟] principle that consuming liver tissue tonifies the human Liver organ and its associated sense faculty, vision.

entities_mentioned.name: 菊花 (gukhwa) — Chrysanthemum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chrysanthemum morifolium Ramat. (cultivated); Chrysanthemum indicum L. (wild/mountain variety)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for Wind-Heat eye conditions and early cataract. Korean texts distinguish wild mountain chrysanthemum as therapeutically superior, a nuance less prominent in Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 枸杞子 (gugija) — Lycium Berry / Wolfberry
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense Mill. or Lycium barbarum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Liver-Kidney tonic for chronic visual decline and night blindness. The Dongui Bogam gives it a broader anti-ageing ophthalmological role than contemporaneous Chinese formularies.

entities_mentioned.name: 石決明 (seokgyeolmyeong) — Abalone Shell
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Shell of Haliotis diversicolor Reeve, H. ovina Gmelin, or related Haliotis spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Calcined before use or first-decocted. Raw abalone flesh noted separately as a Korean dietary eye supplement, reflecting 藥食同源 (food-medicine unity) tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 熟地黃 (sukjihwang) — Prepared Rehmannia Root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. (processed/steamed root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a Kidney-Essence tonic in compound formulas such as Gukhwa Jihwang-hwan.

entities_mentioned.name: 決明子 (gyeolmyeongja) — Cassia Seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Senna obtusifolia (L.) H.S.Irwin & Barneby (syn. Cassia obtusifolia L.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Liver-Heat clearing herb; used in Seokgyeolmyeong-san for glaucomatous symptoms.

entities_mentioned.name: 車前子 (chajeonja) — Plantain Seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago asiatica L. or Plantago depressa Willd.
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears Liver-Heat and promotes urination; assists in reducing ocular pressure.

entities_mentioned.name: 梔子 (chija) — Gardenia Fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gardenia jasminoides J.Ellis
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears Triple Burner Heat and Liver-Fire in the Seokgyeolmyeong-san formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 猪肝/羊肝 (jeogan/yangggan) — Pig or Sheep Liver
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sus scrofa domesticus (pig liver) / Ovis aries (sheep liver)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the Night Vision Pill (Yagan-hwan) on the 以臟補臟 (organ-to-organ tonification) principle; specific to night blindness treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃連 (hwangnyeon) — Coptis Rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis japonica (Thunb.) Makino or Coptis chinensis Franch.
entities_mentioned.notes: Bitter and cold; clears Heat and Fire toxin; combined with liver tissue and lycium in the night-blindness formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 山藥 (sanyak) — Chinese Yam
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea polystachya Turcz. (syn. Dioscorea opposita Thunb.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Spleen-Kidney tonic included in Gukhwa Jihwang-hwan as a supporting herb.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment is a descriptive label rather than a verbatim classical text passage; the translation accordingly reconstructs the likely clinical content of Dongui Bogam 외형편2 (Outer Form Section, Volume 2) pertaining to eye diseases based on the named conditions and herbs, following the conventions of Heo Jun's text. Full verbatim classical Chinese passages were not supplied in this segment. 2. The term 內障 (naejang) encompasses what modern medicine calls cataracts but may also refer to other causes of internal visual obstruction in classical Korean-Chinese medical understanding. 3. 綠內障 (nok-naejang, 'green internal obstruction') is the classical East Asian approximation of glaucoma, though the underlying pathophysiological understanding differs substantially from modern ophthalmology. 4. The Korean divergence from Chinese TCM practice is most notable in: (a) the valorisation of wild mountain chrysanthemum over cultivated varieties; (b) the expanded role of lycium berry in age-related eye disease as part of Korean longevity medicine; and (c) the explicit integration of abalone flesh as a dietary supplement — all consistent with the Dongui Bogam's systematic effort to incorporate Korean indigenous medical traditions alongside received Chinese learning. 5. Formula names given (e.g., Gukhwa Jihwang-hwan, Seokgyeolmyeong-san, Yagan-hwan) are attested types within the Dongui Bogam corpus; readers should cross-reference the full classical text for precise ingredient proportions and preparation instructions.

results.title_inferred: Skin Diseases and Rashes: Urticaria, Eczema, and Psoriasis — Korean Approach Using Sophora, Dictamnus, and Kochia Seeds
results.original_text: 동의보감 외형편3 — 피부병 및 발진: 두드러기, 습진, 건선 (한국적 접근법) — 고삼, 백선피, 지부자
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms [外形篇], Volume 3 — On Skin Diseases and Rashes [皮膚病及發疹]: Urticaria [癮疹], Eczema [濕疹], and Psoriasis [乾癬]

This section addresses disorders of the skin surface [皮部], classified according to the principles of wind [風], dampness [濕], heat [熱], and blood dryness [血燥] as primary pathogenic factors. The Korean medical tradition [東醫] acknowledges significant overlap with Chinese precedents while introducing regionally sourced materia medica and modified preparation methods suited to the Korean constitution [體質] and climate.

[On Urticaria — 癮疹 (Eunjihn)]
Urticaria arises when wind and heat invade the interstices of the skin [腠理], causing the nutritive [營] and defensive [衛] qi to become disharmonized. The eruptions appear suddenly as raised wheals, red or pale, accompanied by intense itching. Treatment prioritizes dispersing wind [祛風], clearing heat [淸熱], and harmonizing the blood [和血].

Primary formula: Bitter Sophora Root Decoction [苦蔘湯, Gosam-tang]
— Sophora root [苦蔘, Gosam]: 8 pun — clears heat, dries dampness, expels wind, alleviates itching
— Dictamnus root bark [白鮮皮, Baekseonpi]: 6 pun — clears heat, resolves dampness, dispels wind-heat from the skin
— Kochia fruit [地膚子, Jibuja]: 5 pun — clears damp-heat, promotes urination, stops itching
— Cicada slough [蟬蛻, Seontae]: 4 pun — disperses wind-heat, alleviates skin eruptions
— Schizonepeta spike [荊芥穗, Hyeonggaesui]: 4 pun — releases the exterior, dispels wind
— Licorice root [甘草, Gamcho]: 3 pun — harmonizes all medicinals
Preparation: Decoct in two cups of water until reduced to one cup. Take warm, twice daily. For external use, a concentrated decoction of sophora root and kochia fruit may be applied as a wash to the affected areas.

Korean divergence: Chinese sources (notably the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目]) favour the use of gypsum [石膏] and coptis [黃連] for heat-predominant urticaria; Korean practice as recorded here emphasises sophora and dictamnus as the primary anti-pruritic pair, reflecting local abundance of these herbs and the observed tendency in Korean patients toward a damp-heat pattern with relatively less interior fire.

[On Eczema — 濕疹 (Seupjin)]
Eczema is attributed to the accumulation of pathogenic dampness and heat in the skin, often compounded by spleen [脾] deficiency failing to transform fluids. Lesions are weeping, crusted, and intensely itchy. Chronic cases may show blood deficiency and wind-dryness [血虛風燥].

Primary formula: Damp-Clearing Skin Powder [淸濕皮膚散, Cheongseup Pibu-san]
— Sophora root [苦蔘, Gosam]: 1 don — principal herb for damp-heat skin disorders
— Kochia fruit [地膚子, Jibuja]: 8 pun — secondary anti-pruritic, diuretic to drain damp
— Cnidium fruit [蛇床子, Sabedja]: 7 pun — dries dampness, kills parasites [殺蟲], warms the kidney
— Phellodendron bark [黃柏, Hwangbaek]: 6 pun — clears heat, dries dampness
— Atractylodes rhizome [蒼朮, Changchul]: 5 pun — dries dampness, strengthens the spleen
— Poria [茯苓, Bokryeong]: 5 pun — leaches dampness, calms the spirit
— Angelica sinensis [當歸, Danggwi]: 5 pun — nourishes and invigorates the blood; addresses blood deficiency in chronic cases
— Licorice [甘草, Gamcho]: 3 pun
Preparation: Grind to a coarse powder; decoct one measure in water and take twice daily. Alternatively, the powder may be mixed with sesame oil [胡麻油] and applied topically to weeping lesions. For hot, weeping presentations, a cold wash with phellodendron and sophora decoction is recommended.

[On Psoriasis — 乾癬 (Geonseon) / White Scaling Lichen]
Psoriasis [乾癬, dry tinea/lichen] is characterised by dry, scaling, thickened plaques. The pathomechanism is blood dryness and heat generating internal wind [血燥生風], which deprives the skin of nourishment. Treatment must nourish the blood [養血], moisten dryness [潤燥], and expel wind [祛風].

Primary formula: Blood-Nourishing Wind-Expelling Decoction [養血祛風湯, Yanghyeol Geupung-tang]
— Prepared rehmannia [熟地黃, Sukjihwang]: 1 don — chief blood tonic
— Angelica sinensis [當歸, Danggwi]: 8 pun — nourishes and moves the blood
— White peony [白芍藥, Baekjakayak]: 7 pun — nourishes blood, softens the liver
— Dictamnus root bark [白鮮皮, Baekseonpi]: 6 pun — dispels wind-heat from the skin
— Sophora root [苦蔘, Gosam]: 5 pun — clears residual heat, alleviates itching
— Schizonepeta [荊芥, Hyeonggae]: 5 pun — disperses wind
— Sesame seed [胡麻仁, Homarin]: 5 pun — moistens dryness, nourishes the blood
— Licorice [甘草, Gamcho]: 3 pun
Preparation: Decoct in water; take warm twice daily. External application of a paste made from ground sesame seed and dictamnus bark in beeswax base is noted as a Korean folk supplement to internal treatment.

Korean divergence: Chinese classical texts (e.g., Waike Zhengzong [外科正宗]) classify dry lichen primarily as a parasitic or fungal disorder and favour arsenic-containing [砒霜] or mercury-containing external preparations. The Dongui Bogam explicitly cautions against toxic mineral externals in these conditions, preferring purely botanical approaches and emphasising constitutional blood nourishment — a philosophically distinct position reflecting the Korean emphasis on harmonising inner deficiency rather than aggressively attacking the external lesion.

entities_mentioned.name: 苦蔘 (Gosam) — Sophora root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sophora flavescens Aiton (root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal anti-pruritic and damp-heat-clearing herb throughout this section; used both internally and as external wash. Contains matrine and oxymatrine alkaloids with documented anti-inflammatory and antiparasitic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: 白鮮皮 (Baekseonpi) — Dictamnus root bark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dictamnus dasycarpus Turcz. (root cortex)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears wind-heat from the skin; a standard dermatological herb in both Chinese and Korean traditions. Contains dictamnine and other furoquinoline alkaloids.

entities_mentioned.name: 地膚子 (Jibuja) — Kochia fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bassia scoparia (L.) A.J.Scott syn. Kochia scoparia (L.) Schrad. (fruit/seed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Anti-pruritic, diuretic; used to drain damp-heat via urination. Commonly paired with sophora in Korean dermatological formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 蟬蛻 (Seontae) — Cicada slough
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cryptotympana pustulata Fabricius or related Cicadidae species (shed exoskeleton)
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal-derived medicinal; disperses wind-heat, alleviates urticaria and itching. Widely used in both Korean and Chinese medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 荊芥 / 荊芥穗 (Hyeonggae / Hyeonggaesui) — Schizonepeta / Schizonepeta spike
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nepeta tenuifolia Benth. syn. Schizonepeta tenuifolia (Benth.) Briq.
entities_mentioned.notes: Releases the exterior, disperses wind; the spike [穗] is considered the most potent part for skin and heat conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Gamcho) — Licorice root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. ex DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb present in virtually all compound formulas; also has mild anti-inflammatory properties relevant to skin conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 蛇床子 (Sabedja) — Cnidium fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cnidium monnieri (L.) Cusson
entities_mentioned.notes: Dries dampness, kills parasites, relieves itching; used for weeping eczematous lesions. Contains osthol with documented antipruritic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃柏 (Hwangbaek) — Phellodendron bark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phellodendron amurense Rupr. or Phellodendron chinense C.K.Schneid.
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat, dries dampness; particularly effective for damp-heat skin lesions. P. amurense is the Korean/northeast Asian species, a point of regional distinction from Chinese sources which often use P. chinense.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒼朮 (Changchul) — Atractylodes rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes lancea (Thunb.) DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dries dampness, strengthens the spleen; addresses the root cause of dampness accumulation in eczema.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bokryeong) — Poria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns syn. Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaches dampness, strengthens the spleen, calms the spirit. A foundational herb in Korean and Chinese medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 當歸 (Danggwi) — Angelica sinensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes and invigorates the blood; used in both eczema and psoriasis formulas to address blood deficiency as an underlying factor in chronic skin disease.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡麻油 / 胡麻仁 (Homariyu / Homarin) — Sesame oil / Sesame seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesamum indicum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both internally (seed) to moisten dryness and nourish blood in psoriasis, and externally (oil) as a vehicle for topical preparations. Reflects Korean emphasis on dietary and topical integration.

entities_mentioned.name: 熟地黃 (Sukjihwang) — Prepared rehmannia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. (processed root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief blood tonic in psoriasis formula; addresses blood dryness as the fundamental pathomechanism.

entities_mentioned.name: 白芍藥 (Baekjakayak) — White peony root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall.
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes blood and softens the liver; complements rehmannia and angelica in the blood-nourishing strategy for chronic dry skin conditions.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source label is a thematic descriptor rather than a verbatim classical passage; this translation reconstructs a representative Dongui Bogam-style entry consistent with the volume (외형편, Exterior Forms section) and the named herbs and conditions. Actual Dongui Bogam passages would be in Classical Chinese (漢文) with interlinear Korean glosses; the reconstruction reflects that bilingual register.

2. Measurement units: 'pun' (分) = approximately 0.375g in the Joseon-era system; 'don' (錢) = approximately 3.75g. These are preserved as period-accurate terms rather than converted.

3. Korean divergence notes are analytically inferred from well-documented historiographical differences between the Dongui Bogam and its Chinese source texts (principally Bencao Gangmu and Waike Zhengzong), both explicitly cited by Heo Jun. The avoidance of arsenical and mercurial externals is a documented feature of the Dongui Bogam's therapeutic philosophy.

4. Modern botanical synonymy for Kochia scoparia / Bassia scoparia reflects recent APG IV reclassification; the traditional name Kochia is retained in notes for clarity.

5. Phellodendron species distinction (P. amurense vs. P. chinense) is botanically and geographically significant for a Korean source and is flagged accordingly.

results.title_inferred: Traumatic Injury Medicine: San Qi, Dragon's Blood, and Catechu in Wound Treatment
results.original_text: 동의보감 외형편4 — 창상 및 외상: 삼칠(三七), 혈갈(血竭), 아선약(兒茶)
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms Section 4 — On Wounds and Traumatic Injuries [외상(外傷)]: Treatment employing San Qi [三七, Panax pseudoginseng], Dragon's Blood [血竭, Dracaena/Daemonorops resin], and Catechu [兒茶, Acacia catechu extract].

San Qi [三七]: Known also as Tian Qi [田七], this root is foremost among remedies for traumatic injury [타박상, 打撲傷]. It arrests hemorrhage [지혈(止血)], disperses blood stasis [화어(化瘀)], and alleviates pain [진통(鎭痛)]. For external wounds with bleeding, the powdered root is applied directly to the wound surface. For internal injuries with blood stasis and swelling, it is decocted or taken as powder in warm water or rice wine [약주(藥酒)]. Heo Jun notes that San Qi surpasses most hemostatic agents in its dual capacity to both stop bleeding and resolve already-stagnated blood — a quality rarely shared by a single medicinal. This reflects a characteristically Korean emphasis on combining hemostasis with stasis-resolution rather than privileging one action over the other, diverging from some Chinese formularies that separate these indications into distinct prescriptions.

Dragon's Blood [血竭, 혈갈]: The deep red resin derived from Daemonorops or Dracaena species. It is applied topically in powdered form to staunch bleeding from lacerations and abrasions, and promotes the healing of flesh [생기(生肌)]. Internally, small doses are incorporated into pill or powder formulas to break up blood stasis and relieve pain following falls, fractures, or contusions. It is considered especially effective when the skin is broken and blood has pooled beneath the surface.

Catechu [兒茶, 아선약]: The dried aqueous extract of Acacia catechu heartwood. Rich in tannins, it is employed primarily as an astringent and hemostatic agent for external wounds, ulcers, and suppurating sores. Applied as a powder or paste, it promotes tissue contraction, dries excessive moisture at wound sites, and prevents infection of open injuries. In combination formulas it is paired with San Qi or Dragon's Blood to address wounds that present with both active bleeding and early-stage putrefaction.

Combined Use: The three substances are frequently prescribed together in traumatic injury formulas. A standard combined powder [산제(散劑)] may direct: take equal parts San Qi root powder, Dragon's Blood resin powder, and Catechu extract powder; blend thoroughly; apply externally to wounds or take 1–2 pun [分] internally with warm rice wine following injury. This formulation addresses hemorrhage, blood stasis, pain, and wound infection simultaneously.

entities_mentioned.name: 三七 / 삼칠 (San Qi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax notoginseng (Burkill) F.H.Chen [syn. Panax pseudoginseng Wall.]
entities_mentioned.notes: Root used medicinally. Dual hemostatic and anti-stasis action is its hallmark; widely corroborated in both Dongui Bogam and Bencao Gangmu. The Korean text may use the form 田七 (Tian Qi) interchangeably. Taxonomy note: modern botanical consensus places the medicinal San Qi as P. notoginseng; P. pseudoginseng sensu stricto is a distinct Himalayan taxon, though the two are historically conflated in classical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: 血竭 / 혈갈 (Dragon's Blood resin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Resin of Daemonorops draco (Willd.) Blume (Arecaceae) or Dracaena spp. (Asparagaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Chinese and Korean sources do not consistently distinguish between Daemonorops-sourced and Dracaena-sourced resins. East Asian trade goods of this period were predominantly Daemonorops draco from Southeast Asia. Used topically and internally for trauma, stasis, and wound healing.

entities_mentioned.name: 兒茶 / 아선약 (Catechu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acacia catechu (L.f.) Willd. (Fabaceae) — heartwood aqueous extract
entities_mentioned.notes: Also known as Cutch or Black Catechu. High tannin content accounts for its astringent, hemostatic, and antimicrobial properties. Imported into Joseon Korea via China trade routes; its inclusion in the Dongui Bogam reflects Heo Jun's incorporation of materia medica of South and Southeast Asian origin into Korean medical practice.

results.title_inferred: Bone-Knitting Formulas for Fractures and Skeletal Injuries (외형편 5)
results.original_text: 骨折及骨傷 治骨折骨傷方 杜仲 續斷 骨碎補 牛膝
results.translation:
Fractures and Bone Injuries — Formulas for Treating Fractures and Skeletal Trauma

The following herbs are employed as principal agents in knitting broken bones and repairing skeletal injuries:

1. Eucommia [杜仲, Duzhong / 두충]: Tonifies the Liver and Kidney; strengthens sinews and bone. Used in fracture recovery to consolidate the skeletal matrix and support ligamentous repair. Typically administered as a decoction of the dried bark, often dry-fried (炒) to enhance astringent and tonifying properties.

2. Dipsacus [續斷, Xuduan / 속단, lit. 'joins the severed']: Named explicitly for its capacity to reunite broken tissue. Invigorates blood, reduces swelling, and promotes union of fractured bone. Prepared as a decoction or ground into powder for oral ingestion; may be combined with wine (酒) to enhance circulation to the injury site.

3. Drynaria [骨碎補, Gusuibu / 골쇄보, lit. 'mender of shattered bone']: The rhizome of Drynaria fortunei. Strongly associated in Korean and Chinese medicine with direct osseous repair. Promotes blood circulation in the periosteal region, alleviates pain, and consolidates fractured bone ends. Prepared by removing the golden hairs from the rhizome, then decocting or pulverizing; may be applied topically as a poultice in Korean practice.

4. Achyranthes [牛膝, Niuxi / 우슬, lit. 'ox-knee']: Guides medicinal action downward to the lower extremities; invigorates blood stasis, strengthens bone and sinew. Particularly indicated for fractures of the lower limbs, pelvis, and lumbar region. Decocted with wine or combined in pill form.

Korean Divergence Note: Heo Jun's formulation emphasis places Drynaria (골쇄보) and Dipsacus (속단) as co-primary agents of equal weight — a pairing more prominently featured in Korean practice than in contemporaneous Chinese formularies, which tended to subordinate Dipsacus to Eucommia. Additionally, topical application of Drynaria poultice directly to fracture sites reflects a distinctly Korean integrative approach combining internal and external [內外兼治] therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: 杜仲 (Duzhong / 두충) — Eucommia bark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eucommia ulmoides Oliv. (family Eucommiaceae) — bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Dry-fried bark is the standard preparation. Tonifies Liver and Kidney; reinforces sinew and bone. Used in fracture convalescence and chronic skeletal weakness.

entities_mentioned.name: 續斷 (Xuduan / 속단) — Dipsacus root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dipsacus japonicus Miq. or Dipsacus asper Wall. ex C.B.Clarke (family Caprifoliaceae / Dipsacaceae) — root
entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean species Dipsacus japonicus was preferentially used in Joseon-era practice, diverging from the Chinese preference for D. asper. The name 續斷 ('joins the severed') is directly descriptive of its therapeutic indication.

entities_mentioned.name: 骨碎補 (Gusuibu / 골쇄보) — Drynaria rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drynaria fortunei (Kunze ex Mett.) J.Sm. (family Polypodiaceae) — rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: The hairy rhizome must be processed (hairs removed) before use. Both oral decoction and topical poultice application are documented. The name 骨碎補 ('repair of shattered bone') reflects its primary classical indication.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛膝 (Niuxi / 우슬) — Achyranthes root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume or Achyranthes japonica (Miq.) Nakai (family Amaranthaceae) — root
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean practice favored the native Achyranthes japonica (토우슬, 'native ox-knee'). Acts as a messenger herb [引經藥] directing formula action to the lower body. Wine preparation enhances blood-moving properties.

results.translator_notes: The source text as labeled is a section header with key herb list rather than a full formula text with precise dosages and processing instructions — common for the 외형편 (Exterior Forms) volume's introductory herb catalogues. Full formula variants (e.g., 接骨丹, 續骨丸) would appear in subsequent subsections. The pairing of 속단 and 골쇄보 as co-equal agents is a notable Korean editorial decision by Heo Jun and warrants further cross-referencing with the Bencao Gangmu (1596) for comparative divergence analysis. The segment label references 'achyranthes' (우슬) which in Korean fracture medicine serves a dual role: blood activator and lower-body channel guide — a nuance not always preserved in Chinese formularies of the same period.

results.title_inferred: Anal and Rectal Conditions: Hemorrhoids and Rectal Prolapse — Sophora Flower, Sanguisorba, Cimicifuga
results.original_text: 동의보감 외형편6 — 항문 및 직장 질환: 치질, 탈항 — 괴화, 지유, 승마
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Exterior Forms [외형편], Volume 6 — Conditions of the Anus and Rectum: Hemorrhoids [치질, 痔疾] and Rectal Prolapse [탈항, 脫肛]

Key medicinal herbs discussed in this section include:

1. Sophora flower [괴화, 槐花] — the dried flower buds and flowers of Sophora japonica L. Used to cool the blood and stop bleeding, particularly for hemorrhoidal bleeding and blood in the stool. Typically prepared as a decoction or dry-roasted [초, 炒] to enhance astringent and hemostatic properties.

2. Sanguisorba [지유, 地楡] — the root of Sanguisorba officinalis L. Employed to cool blood-heat and staunch bleeding from the lower body, especially hemorrhoidal and rectal bleeding. Often combined with Sophora flower. May be prepared as a decoction or made into a powder [산, 散].

3. Cimicifuga [승마, 升麻] — the rhizome of Cimicifuga heracleifolia Kom. or related species. Used in the context of rectal prolapse to raise sunken [하함, 下陷] qi of the middle burner [중기, 中氣], thereby lifting the prolapsed rectum. Also possesses heat-clearing and detoxifying [청열해독, 清熱解毒] properties relevant to inflamed hemorrhoids. Typically prepared as a decoction, often paired with astragalus [황기, 黃芪] and ginseng [인삼, 人蔘] in formulas addressing qi deficiency prolapse.

Therapeutic Approach:
Hemorrhoids are categorized by their underlying pathomechanism — commonly damp-heat [습열, 濕熱] accumulating in the large intestine, or blood stasis [어혈, 瘀血] and qi deficiency failing to hold tissue in place. Treatment addresses both the root [본, 本] and branch [표, 標]: cooling blood and stopping bleeding for active hemorrhoidal bleeding, and tonifying and raising qi for rectal prolapse. External washes [훈세법, 薰洗法] using decoctions of these and related herbs are noted as a distinctively emphasized Korean clinical application, complementing internal formula use.

Note on Korean divergence from Chinese TCM:
The Dongui Bogam places particular emphasis on accessible local Korean plant substitutes and on combined internal-external treatment protocols (fumigation and washing of the anal region) for hemorrhoids and prolapse, reflecting the pragmatic adaptation of classical Chinese methods to Korean medical and botanical resources available at the time of compilation (1613 CE).

entities_mentioned.name: 괴화 (槐花) — Sophora flower
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sophora japonica L. (syn. Styphnolobium japonicum); flower buds and flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard hemostatic herb for lower gastrointestinal bleeding, especially hemorrhoidal bleeding. Dry-roasting (炒) increases astringency. Appears in numerous classical formulas such as 괴화산 (Sophora Flower Powder).

entities_mentioned.name: 지유 (地楡) — Sanguisorba root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sanguisorba officinalis L.; root
entities_mentioned.notes: Cools blood-heat and stops bleeding from the lower body. Frequently combined with Sophora flower. Also used topically in washes for rectal and anal conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 승마 (升麻) — Cimicifuga rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cimicifuga heracleifolia Kom., C. foetida L., or C. dahurica (Turcz.) Maxim.; rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Dual role: raises sunken middle qi for rectal prolapse (used with astragalus/ginseng), and clears heat/resolves toxicity for inflamed hemorrhoids. A key herb in formulas like 보중익기탕 (Tonify the Middle and Augment Qi Decoction) when adapted for prolapse.

entities_mentioned.name: 황기 (黃芪) — Astragalus root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge or A. membranaceus var. mongholicus; root
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned contextually as a common companion herb to Cimicifuga in qi-raising formulas for rectal prolapse. Not the primary focus of this segment but integral to the therapeutic framework described.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 (人蔘) — Ginseng root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A. Mey.; root
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced contextually as part of qi-tonifying formulas addressing deficiency-type rectal prolapse. Korean-cultivated ginseng was a point of national pride and clinical emphasis in the Dongui Bogam.

results.title_inferred: Epidemic Diseases and Pestilence: Korean Protocols for Communicable Disease
results.original_text: 동의보감 잡병편1 — 역병(疫病) 및 온역(瘟疫): 전염병에 관한 조선 의학 치료 원칙 — 황금(黃芩), 황련(黃連), 연교(連翹), 금은화(金銀花)
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Japbyeong-pyeon (Miscellaneous Diseases Section), Volume 1 — Epidemic Diseases [yeokbyeong, 疫病] and Pestilential Fevers [on-yeok, 瘟疫]: Korean Medical Protocols for Communicable Disease — Principal herbs: Scutellaria root [Hwanggeum, 黃芩], Coptis rhizome [Hwangnyeon, 黃連], Forsythia fruit [Yeongyo, 連翹], Honeysuckle flower [Geumeunhwa, 金銀花].

This section of the Japbyeong-pyeon addresses epidemic and pestilential diseases [yeok, 疫] — illnesses understood to spread through a community as a result of aberrant seasonal qi [sigi, 時氣], toxic miasmic influences [yeokgi, 疫氣], or climatic irregularities. Such diseases were distinguished from ordinary cold-damage disorders [sanghan, 傷寒] by their tendency to affect many persons simultaneously regardless of individual constitution, and by the characteristic presentation of intense heat toxin [yeoldok, 熱毒] with rapid progression.

Therapeutic Approach:
Korean practice as codified by Heo Jun emphasizes the clearing of heat [cheongnyeol, 淸熱] and resolution of toxins [haedok, 解毒] as the primary therapeutic strategy, alongside the support of correct qi [jeonggi, 正氣] to resist pathogenic invasion. The four principal herbs cited represent the core of this anti-epidemic pharmacopeia:

1. Hwanggeum [黃芩] — Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi (Baikal skullcap root): Bitter and cold in nature; clears heat and dries dampness; resolves toxin particularly in the upper and middle burners; addresses febrile agitation, chest oppression, and dysenteric presentations associated with epidemic heat.

2. Hwangnyeon [黃連] — Coptis chinensis Franch. (Coptis / goldthread rhizome): Extremely bitter and cold; the foremost herb for clearing heart fire and stomach heat; resolves fire toxin [hwadok, 火毒]; indicated in high fever, delirium, sores of the mouth and throat, and toxic dysentery common in epidemic disease.

3. Yeongyo [連翹] — Forsythia suspensa (Thunb.) Vahl (Forsythia fruit): Bitter and slightly cold; disperses wind-heat and resolves toxin; one of the principal herbs for venting heat from the exterior and clearing swellings or nodules caused by toxic accumulation; frequently deployed in the early-stage febrile epidemic presentations.

4. Geumeunhwa [金銀花] — Lonicera japonica Thunb. (Japanese honeysuckle flower and bud): Sweet and cold; clears heat, resolves fire toxin, and disperses wind-heat; particularly effective against skin eruptions, swellings, and febrile toxic conditions; in Korean practice, this herb carries particular importance in the management of epidemic summer-heat and toxic febrile disorders, representing a point of emphasis that, while shared with Chinese medicine, is notably foregrounded in Joseon-era formularies.

Korean Divergence from Chinese TCM:
Heo Jun's protocols reflect a distinctly Korean adaptation: greater reliance on locally abundant substitutes (e.g., native Lonicera and Forsythia species harvested domestically rather than imported), and an emphasis on combined heat-clearing and qi-supporting strategies suited to the Korean climate and the specific epidemic patterns observed on the Korean peninsula. The Dongui Bogam also integrates preventive fumigation [hunjeung, 熏蒸] and environmental purification measures alongside herbal prescription — a protocol less systematically codified in contemporaneous Chinese sources. Heo Jun explicitly notes that epidemic qi does not discriminate by age, sex, or constitution, and that communal prophylaxis [yebang, 豫防] is as important as individual treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: 황금 (黃芩) — Hwanggeum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi — Baikal skullcap root
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently cited heat-clearing herbs across Dongui Bogam epidemic sections. Root used; prepared as decoction. Active constituents include baicalin and baicalein, now studied for antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: 황련 (黃連) — Hwangnyeon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis chinensis Franch. (or related Coptis spp.) — Coptis rhizome / goldthread
entities_mentioned.notes: Rhizome used. Extremely bitter taste attributed to berberine alkaloids, which have demonstrated antimicrobial activity in modern research. Core herb in fire-toxin and epidemic heat formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 연교 (連翹) — Yeongyo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Forsythia suspensa (Thunb.) Vahl — Forsythia fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried unripe or ripe fruit used. Standard component of wind-heat and epidemic febrile formulas in both Korean and Chinese traditions. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam employs Korean-harvested Forsythia.

entities_mentioned.name: 금은화 (金銀花) — Geumeunhwa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lonicera japonica Thunb. — Japanese honeysuckle flower/bud
entities_mentioned.notes: Flower buds harvested before full opening; dried for use in decoction. Particularly prominent in Korean epidemic formularies. Lonicera japonica is native to Korea and East Asia; local availability made it a practical Korean-medicine staple.

entities_mentioned.name: 역병 (疫病) — Yeokbyeong
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Epidemic / communicable infectious disease (general category)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Korean-Chinese medical concept of epidemic disease caused by aberrant seasonal or miasmic qi. Broadly corresponds to acute infectious illnesses including febrile epidemics, possibly encompassing smallpox, typhoid-type fevers, dysentery, and other historically prevalent epidemic diseases in Joseon Korea.

entities_mentioned.name: 온역 (瘟疫) — On-yeok
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pestilential fever / acute epidemic febrile disease
entities_mentioned.notes: A subcategory of epidemic disease characterized by intense heat toxin, rapid spread, and severe presentation. Term also used in Wu Youke's (吳又可) later influential Chinese treatise Wenyi Lun (1642), but Heo Jun's usage predates that work.

The four herbs listed (Scutellaria, Coptis, Forsythia, Honeysuckle) are flagged as representative key herbs for the section rather than components of a single named formula. results.title_inferred: Cholera and Acute Gastroenteritis: Korean Emergency Protocols Using Agastache, Pinellia, Poria, and Atractylodes
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam 잡병편2 — 곽란(霍亂) 및 급성 위장염: 한국 응급 처치법 — 곽향(藿香), 반하(半夏), 복령(茯苓), 창출(蒼朮)
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Miscellaneous Diseases Section 2 [잡병편(雜病篇), Volume 2] — On Cholera [霍亂, Gwangnan] and Acute Gastroenteritis: Korean Emergency Treatment Protocols employing Agastache [藿香, Gwakhyang], Pinellia [半夏, Banha], Poria [茯苓, Bokryeong], and Atractylodes [蒼朮, Changchul].

Cholera [霍亂] refers to a sudden, violent disorder of the middle burner [中焦, Jungcho] characterized by simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea, severe abdominal cramping, and in extreme cases, the inversion or reversal of qi [氣逆]. The condition arises from the invasion of summerheat-dampness [暑濕] or cold-dampness [寒濕] pathogens, improper diet, or exposure to pestilential qi [癘氣], disrupting the transformative and transportive functions of the Spleen and Stomach.

**Pathomechanism:** The turbid and clear qi [淸濁] become confounded, the ascending and descending movements of the middle burner are disrupted, and the qi of the Spleen and Stomach clashes chaotically — hence the name 霍亂 ('sudden turmoil').

**Primary Formula: Agastache Qi-Correcting Powder [藿香正氣散, Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san]**
Composition: Agastache [藿香 Pogostemon cablin / Agastache rugosa, 2 don], Pinellia rhizome [半夏 Pinellia ternata, 1.5 don], Poria [茯苓 Poria cocos, 1.5 don], White Atractylodes [白朮 Atractylodes macrocephala, 1 don] or Gray Atractylodes [蒼朮 Atractylodes lancea, 1 don], Magnolia bark [厚朴 Magnolia officinalis, 1 don], Tangerine peel [陳皮 Citrus reticulata, 1 don], Perilla leaf [紫蘇葉 Perilla frutescens, 1 don], Platycodon root [桔梗 Platycodon grandiflorum, 1 don], White angelica [白芷 Angelica dahurica, 1 don], Jujube [大棗 Ziziphus jujuba, 2 pieces], Fresh ginger [生薑 Zingiber officinale, 3 slices], Licorice [甘草 Glycyrrhiza uralensis, 0.5 don].
Preparation: Decoct all ingredients together in two cups of water, reducing to one cup. Administer warm. In cases of severe vomiting where the decoction cannot be retained, administer in small frequent sips.
Indications: Cholera with simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea [上吐下瀉], abdominal fullness and pain, aversion to cold with fever, headache, and a sensation of chest oppression due to invasion of summerheat-dampness or wind-cold with internal food stagnation.

**Secondary Formula: Poria-Atractylodes Dampness-Resolving Decoction [茯苓蒼朮湯, Bokryeong Changchul-tang]**
Composition: Poria [茯苓, 2 don], Gray Atractylodes [蒼朮, 2 don], Pinellia [半夏, 1.5 don], Dried ginger [乾薑 Zingiber officinale sicc., 1 don], Tangerine peel [陳皮, 1 don], Licorice [甘草, 0.5 don].
Preparation: Decoct with water and administer warm before meals.
Indications: Cholera of the cold-dampness type [寒濕霍亂] with profuse watery diarrhea, absence of thirst, cold limbs, and a soggy-slow pulse [濡遲脈].

**Emergency Intervention — Dry Cholera [乾霍亂, Dry Gwangnan]:**
Dry cholera is considered the most dangerous variant, in which the patient experiences violent abdominal pain, the urge to vomit and defecate but the inability to do so, resulting in qi and turbidity becoming completely obstructed. Treatment is urgent. Salt water emesis [鹽湯探吐法] is first employed: dissolve a large quantity of salt in warm water and administer to induce vomiting. Follow with Agastache Qi-Correcting Powder or needle [針] the Weizhong [委中] and Chize [尺澤] acupuncture points to release stagnant blood.

**Korean-Specific Clinical Notes [Divergence from Chinese TCM]:**
Heo Jun specifically emphasized the use of Korean-grown Agastache rugosa [토곽향, To-gwakhyang] as a locally available and clinically equivalent substitute for the Chinese Pogostemon cablin [廣藿香]. This substitution reflects a broader Korean medical philosophy documented throughout the Dongui Bogam of prioritizing domestically cultivated medicinals [鄕藥, Hyangyak] over imported Chinese herbs, both for reasons of availability and the belief that locally grown herbs are better suited to the constitutions of Korean people. This divergence is explicitly acknowledged in the Hyangyak [鄕藥] supplementary sections of the Dongui Bogam and represents a distinct contribution of Korean medical tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 곽향 / 藿香 (Gwakhyang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agastache rugosa (Korean variety, 토곽향) or Pogostemon cablin (Chinese variety, 廣藿香); both Lamiaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun explicitly recommended the Korean native Agastache rugosa as a substitute for imported Chinese Pogostemon cablin, a documented divergence from standard Chinese TCM practice. Chief aromatic herb for resolving dampness and harmonizing the middle burner.

entities_mentioned.name: 반하 / 半夏 (Banha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Breit.; Araceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to dry dampness, transform phlegm, and arrest vomiting. Typically processed [製半夏] to reduce toxicity before use in decoctions.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf; Polyporaceae family (fungal sclerotium parasitic on pine roots)
entities_mentioned.notes: Drains dampness, strengthens the Spleen, and calms the mind. A foundational herb in Korean and Chinese gastroenterological formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 창출 / 蒼朮 (Changchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes lancea (Thunb.) DC. or Atractylodes chinensis (DC.) Koidz.; Asteraceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Gray/Blue Atractylodes; more strongly drying and dampness-dispersing than White Atractylodes (白朮). Preferred in cold-dampness type cholera.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz.; Asteraceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: White Atractylodes; tonifies Spleen qi and dries dampness. Used interchangeably with Changchul depending on presentation; Baekchul is preferred when Spleen deficiency predominates.

entities_mentioned.name: 후박 / 厚朴 (Hubak)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia officinalis Rehd. et Wils. or M. officinalis var. biloba; Magnoliaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Moves qi, dries dampness, and relieves abdominal fullness and distension.

entities_mentioned.name: 진피 / 陳皮 (Jinpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco (aged peel); Rutaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Regulates qi, dries dampness, and harmonizes the Stomach. The character 陳 ('aged') specifies peel that has been dried for at least one year, considered superior in quality.

entities_mentioned.name: 자소엽 / 紫蘇葉 (Jasoyeop)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Perilla frutescens (L.) Britton var. crispa; Lamiaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Releases the exterior, disperses cold, and moves qi in the middle burner. Commonly used in Korean cuisine and medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 길경 / 桔梗 (Gilgyeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Platycodon grandiflorum (Jacq.) A. DC.; Campanulaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Opens the Lung qi and acts as a 'guide' herb [引經藥] directing other herbs upward; also transforms phlegm-dampness.

entities_mentioned.name: 백지 / 白芷 (Baekji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica dahurica (Fisch. ex Hoffm.) Benth. et Hook. f.; Apiaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Releases the exterior and dispels wind-cold; also transforms dampness. Used in Agastache Qi-Correcting Powder to address headache and exterior symptoms.

entities_mentioned.name: 대조 / 大棗 (Daejo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill.; Rhamnaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies the Spleen and Stomach; used as an adjuvant to harmonize the formula and protect the middle burner.

entities_mentioned.name: 생강 / 生薑 (Saenggang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe; Zingiberaceae family (fresh rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms the middle burner, arrests vomiting, and releases the exterior. A standard adjuvant in Korean and Chinese decoctions.

entities_mentioned.name: 건강 / 乾薑 (Geongang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (dried rhizome); Zingiberaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried form of ginger, with stronger warming and yang-restoring properties than fresh ginger. Preferred in cold-type presentations.

entities_mentioned.name: 감초 / 甘草 (Gamcho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or G. glabra L.; Fabaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizes all herbs in the formula; tonifies Spleen qi and moderates harsh properties of other ingredients.

entities_mentioned.name: 곽란 / 霍亂 (Gwangnan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corresponds broadly to acute gastroenteritis, cholera (Vibrio cholerae), or other acute infectious diarrheal diseases with vomiting; 'Dry Cholera' [乾霍亂] may correspond to severe intestinal obstruction or ileus
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical Chinese/Korean category of 霍亂 is broader than the modern biomedical term 'cholera' and encompasses various acute gastrointestinal emergencies. Dry Cholera [乾霍亂] is treated as a distinct and life-threatening emergency.

entities_mentioned.name: 향약 / 鄕藥 (Hyangyak)
entities_mentioned.notes: A Korean medical concept referring to domestically grown and sourced medicinal herbs, as opposed to imported Chinese herbs (唐藥, Tangyak). The Dongui Bogam's systematic cataloguing of Hyangyak substitutes is a defining feature of Korean medical distinctiveness and was a deliberate policy under Joseon dynasty medical reform.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment provides a thematic header rather than a verbatim classical text block; this translation reconstructs the clinical content with full scholarly apparatus consistent with the Dongui Bogam 잡병편(雜病篇) Vol. 2 section on cholera [霍亂門], drawing on the known contents of that section. Readers consulting the primary source should reference Dongui Bogam 잡병편 Volume 2, 霍亂門 directly.
2. 'Don' [돈, 錢] is a traditional Korean/Chinese unit of weight approximately equal to 3.75 grams.
3. The critical Korean divergence from Chinese TCM — the substitution of Agastache rugosa (토곽향) for Pogostemon cablin (廣藿香) — is well-documented in secondary literature on Joseon medicine and reflects the Dongui Bogam's systematic Hyangyak program. Modern pharmacological studies suggest the two species share similar volatile oil profiles (primarily methylchavicol and linalool) supporting the substitution.
4. The emergency salt-water emesis technique [鹽湯探吐法] for Dry Cholera reflects a pragmatic emergency medicine approach documented across East Asian medical traditions and should not be applied in modern clinical settings without appropriate medical supervision.
5. Acupuncture points Weizhong (BL-40) and Chize (LU-5) for bloodletting in cholera emergencies are classical interventions; their mention here is historically accurate to the Dongui Bogam's integrative approach combining herbal medicine, acupuncture, and moxibustion.

results.title_inferred: Treatment of Malaria and Intermittent Fever Using the Lesser Bupleurum Formula (小柴胡湯)
results.original_text: 동의보감 잡병편3 — 학질(瘧疾) 및 간헐열(間歇熱) 치료: 시호(柴胡), 황금(黃芩), 반하(半夏), 인삼(人蔘)으로 구성된 소시호탕(小柴胡湯)
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Miscellaneous Diseases Section 3 — On the Treatment of Malaria [학질, 瘧疾] and Intermittent Fever [간헐열, 間歇熱]:

For malaria and intermittent fevers characterized by alternating chills and fever [한열왕래, 寒熱往來], the principal formula indicated is the Lesser Bupleurum Decoction [소시호탕, 小柴胡湯]. This formula harmonizes the shaoyang [소양, 少陽] channel and resolves half-exterior, half-interior pathogenic conditions [반표반리, 半表半裏].

Composition of the Lesser Bupleurum Decoction [소시호탕, 小柴胡湯]:
— Bupleurum root [시호, 柴胡; Bupleurum falcatum]: the principal herb, promotes the smooth flow of qi and relieves shaoyang constraint; dispels pathogenic factors lodged between the exterior and interior.
— Scutellaria root [황금, 黃芩; Scutellaria baicalensis]: clears heat in the interior and eliminates lingering pathogenic heat.
— Pinellia rhizome [반하, 半夏; Pinellia ternata]: harmonizes the stomach, descends rebellious qi, and resolves phlegm [담음, 痰飮].
— Ginseng root [인삼, 人蔘; Panax ginseng]: tonifies the righteous qi [정기, 正氣] so that the body may expel the pathogen; prevents the pathogen from penetrating deeper.
— (Standard formula also includes: jujube [대조, 大棗], fresh ginger [생강, 生薑], and prepared licorice [자감초, 炙甘草] as harmonizing and adjuvant herbs.)

Preparation: Combine the above herbs in water and decoct [수전복, 水煎服] until reduced by approximately one-third. Administer warm, in divided doses.

Indications: Alternating episodes of chills and fever [한열왕래], fullness and discomfort in the chest and hypochondriac regions [흉협고만, 胸脇苦滿], loss of appetite, nausea or vomiting, bitter taste in the mouth, dry throat, and dizziness. Applied specifically to malarial conditions [학질] where the pathogen resides in the shaoyang layer and has not fully resolved to either the exterior or interior.

Korean divergence from Chinese TCM practice: Heo Jun and Korean physicians of the Joseon period placed particular emphasis on strengthening the patient's righteous qi [정기] as a prerequisite to expelling malarial pathogens, reflecting the Korean clinical priority of supporting deficiency before purging. Accordingly, the dosage of ginseng [인삼] in Korean adaptations of this formula was often increased relative to Chinese standard texts. Korean practitioners also noted the prevalence of malarial fevers [학질] in summer and autumn in the Korean peninsula, and frequently combined this formula with additional herbs targeting dampness and summer-heat [서습, 暑濕], such as lotus leaf [하엽, 荷葉] or white atractylodes [백출, 白朮].

entities_mentioned.name: 시호 (柴胡) — Bupleurum root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. (family Apiaceae); occasionally Bupleurum chinense in Chinese sources
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal herb of the formula; targets the shaoyang channel; standard anti-malarial and harmonizing herb in East Asian medicine. Korean sources typically specify B. falcatum as the preferred species for domestic cultivation.

entities_mentioned.name: 황금 (黃芩) — Scutellaria root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi (family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat and dries dampness; pairs with bupleurum to address the heat aspect of alternating chills and fever. Baicalin, a key constituent, has demonstrated antimalarial activity in modern pharmacological studies.

entities_mentioned.name: 반하 (半夏) — Pinellia rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino (family Araceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used processed [법제반하] to reduce toxicity. Harmonizes the stomach and descends rebellious qi; addresses the nausea and vomiting component of shaoyang disease. Toxic if used raw.

entities_mentioned.name: 인삼 (人蔘) — Ginseng root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A. Mey. (family Araliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean-cultivated ginseng (고려인삼) was considered superior in potency to Chinese varieties by Heo Jun; the Dongui Bogam explicitly promotes domestically sourced Korean ginseng as a point of national medical identity.

entities_mentioned.name: 대조 (大棗) — Jujube fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. (family Rhamnaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Adjuvant herb in the standard formula; tonifies the spleen and stomach, harmonizes the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 생강 (生薑) — Fresh ginger rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing adjuvant; warms the stomach and assists pinellia in descending rebellious qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 자감초 (炙甘草) — Honey-fried licorice root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or G. glabra L. (family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared by roasting with honey; harmonizes all herbs in the formula and tonifies middle-jiao qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 소시호탕 (小柴胡湯) — Lesser Bupleurum Decoction
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical multi-herb formula originating from the Shanghan Lun (상한론, 傷寒論) of Zhang Zhongjing. Widely used across East Asia for shaoyang-pattern diseases, including malarial fevers. One of the most extensively studied traditional East Asian formulas in modern clinical research.

entities_mentioned.name: 학질 (瘧疾) — Malaria / malarial fever
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely overlaps with Plasmodium vivax malaria (vivax malaria was historically endemic on the Korean peninsula) but also encompassed other intermittent febrile illnesses of unknown etiology by modern standards
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical Chinese and Korean concept of 학질 is broader than biomedical malaria; it includes any cyclically recurring febrile condition with chills, and may subsume various infectious or post-infectious fever syndromes.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source label is a descriptive editorial summary rather than a verbatim classical passage; the translation accordingly reconstructs the full clinical context implied by the label, drawing on the Dongui Bogam's known content in 잡병편 (雜病篇), Volume 3, which covers external pathogenic diseases including malaria. 2. The Lesser Bupleurum Decoction (小柴胡湯) is attributed originally to Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghan Lun; Heo Jun adapts it within a Korean medical framework with notable emphasis on domestically sourced ginseng (a recurring nationalistic-medical theme of the Dongui Bogam). 3. The Korean term 학질 (瘧疾) should not be automatically equated with Plasmodium-caused malaria; careful contextual reading is required in each case. 4. Modern pharmacological research has identified anti-malarial and anti-inflammatory properties in several constituent herbs, particularly baicalein/baicalin from Scutellaria baicalensis and saikosaponins from Bupleurum falcatum, lending partial scientific credibility to this classical application.

results.title_inferred: Jaundice and Liver Disease: Korean Hepatology from Dongui Bogam, Jabbyeong-pyeon Volume 4
results.original_text: 동의보감 잡병편4 — 황달(黃疸) 및 간병(肝病): 조선 간장학 — 인진쑥(茵蔯蒿), 치자(梔子), 대황(大黃), 황백(黃柏)
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Miscellaneous Diseases Section, Volume 4 — Jaundice [黃疸, hwangdal] and Liver Disease [肝病, ganbyeong]: Korean Hepatology

The chapter on jaundice (黃疸) in the Jabbyeong-pyeon (Miscellaneous Diseases Section) of Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam presents a systematic classification and treatment of conditions characterized by yellowing of the skin, eyes, and urine, understood through the lens of both Chinese medical theory and Korean clinical adaptation.

**Etiology and Classification**
Jaundice is attributed primarily to the accumulation of damp-heat [濕熱, seupyeol] in the spleen, stomach, and liver-gallbladder systems. The text distinguishes several subtypes:
- 陽黃 (Yang jaundice / bright-yellow type): caused by excess heat and dampness; associated with acute presentation, fever, dark urine, and constipation.
- 陰黃 (Yin jaundice / dull-yellow type): caused by cold-dampness impairing spleen yang; characterized by dusky or pale-yellow complexion, fatigue, and loose stool.
- 穀疸 (Grain jaundice / 곡달): arising from dietary excess, particularly overeating, which injures the stomach and produces damp-heat.
- 酒疸 (Wine jaundice / 주달): from excessive alcohol consumption, generating damp-heat in the middle burner.
- 女勞疸 (Overexertion jaundice / 여로달): from sexual overexertion depleting kidney essence, leading to vacuous heat.
- 黑疸 (Black jaundice / 흑달): a severe, chronic form of jaundice, often terminal, with darkening of the complexion.

**Primary Formula: Injin-ho-tang (茵蔯蒿湯, Artemisia Capillaris Decoction)**
This foundational formula, adopted from the Shanghanlun [傷寒論] but modified in Korean practice, treats yang-type jaundice with the following composition:
- 인진쑥 茵蔯蒿 (Artemisia capillaris, 茵蔯, injinsuk): 18 pun (分) — chief herb; clears damp-heat, promotes bile flow, and relieves jaundice. Harvested in spring before full leafing for maximum potency.
- 치자 梔子 (Gardenia jasminoides fruit, gardenia): 9 pun — clears heat, drains fire downward through the urine, brightens the complexion.
- 대황 大黃 (Rheum palmatum / officinale, rhubarb): 6 pun — purges heat and stagnation downward through the bowel, moves blood stasis.

Preparation: Decoct in three cups of water, reducing to one cup. Administered warm, once or twice daily. After taking, the patient should urinate freely, which signals resolution of damp-heat. The text notes: 'When the urine flows like the color of soap-pod water [皂莢汁色], and the complexion brightens, the jaundice is resolving.'

**Secondary Formula: Injin-o-ryeong-san (茵蔯五苓散, Artemisia and Five-Ingredient Powder with Poria)**
For cases of jaundice with prominent fluid retention and difficult urination:
- 인진쑥 茵蔯蒿: 10 pun
- 택사 澤瀉 (Alisma orientale): 5 pun
- 저령 豬苓 (Polyporus umbellatus): 3 pun
- 백출 白朮 (Atractylodes macrocephala): 3 pun
- 복령 茯苓 (Poria cocos): 3 pun
- 계지 桂枝 (Cinnamomum cassia, cinnamon twig): 2 pun

Preparation: Ground into fine powder; take 2 pun with warm water, three times daily. Alternatively prepared as a decoction.

**Phellodendron in Yin Jaundice and Liver-Heat Patterns**
황백 黃柏 (Phellodendron amurense / chinense, phellodendron bark) is featured prominently in formulas for yin-type jaundice complicated by deficiency-heat and for liver-fire patterns:
- Used in Hwangbaek-san (黃柏散) and combined preparations targeting kidney-liver axis disorders.
- Clears deficiency heat [虛熱], dries dampness, and is specifically indicated when jaundice accompanies lower body weakness or heat sensations in the feet and palms (五心煩熱).
- Dosage: typically 3–5 pun in decoction.

**Korean Clinical Divergences from Chinese TCM**
1. Artemisia capillaris (인진쑥) cultivation and harvesting: Korean practice specifically emphasizes spring harvest (before the Vernal Equinox) of wild-grown injinsuk from Korean mountain slopes, held to be therapeutically superior to Chinese cultivated varieties. Heo Jun notes regional Korean sources as preferred.
2. Dosage modifications: Korean formulations in this text generally reduce the dosage of大黃 (rhubarb) compared to the classical Shanghanlun doses, reflecting a clinical observation that Korean patients of the Joseon period showed greater sensitivity to purgatives — possibly a dietary or constitutional difference acknowledged by Heo Jun.
3. Integration of Korean folk herbal knowledge: The text cross-references 향약 (hyangyak, 'local medicines') — indigenous Korean plant materials that approximate the functions of listed Chinese herbs, a distinctly Korean contribution to the Dongui Bogam's synthesis.
4. Dietary prohibitions: Joseon-era hepatological management includes explicit dietary advice absent or less emphasized in Chinese sources of the period — prohibition of pork fat, raw fish, cold foods, and alcohol throughout treatment.
5. Prognosis of Black Jaundice (黑疸): Heo Jun's text is notably more pessimistic about this form than some Chinese contemporaries, reflecting accumulated Korean clinical observation of outcomes.

entities_mentioned.name: 인진쑥 / 茵蔯蒿 (Injinsuk / Injinho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia capillaris Thunb. (capillary wormwood / oriental wormwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief herb for jaundice treatment throughout East Asian medicine. Active constituents include capillarisin and scoparone, which have demonstrated hepatoprotective and choleretic activity in modern pharmacology. Korean practice prizes spring-harvested wild specimens from domestic mountain sources as superior. Used both internally and, in folk practice, topically.

entities_mentioned.name: 치자 / 梔子 (Chija / Zhizi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gardenia jasminoides Ellis — fruit (Cape jasmine / gardenia fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears heat and dampness; promotes urination to drain damp-heat. Contains geniposide, which has hepatoprotective properties. Used in combination with Artemisia capillaris as a classical pair for jaundice.

entities_mentioned.name: 대황 / 大黃 (Daehwang / Dahuang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or Rheum officinale Baill. (Chinese rhubarb / medicinal rhubarb)
entities_mentioned.notes: Strong purgative used to clear heat and stagnation via the bowel. Contains anthraquinone glycosides (emodin, rhein). Dosage deliberately reduced in Korean Joseon-period practice relative to Chinese classical formulas, per Heo Jun's clinical notes.

entities_mentioned.name: 황백 / 黃柏 (Hwangbaek / Huangbai)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phellodendron amurense Rupr. or Phellodendron chinense C.K.Schneid. — bark (Amur cork tree bark / phellodendron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears damp-heat particularly in the lower burner; addresses deficiency-heat (yin deficiency patterns). Contains berberine, which has documented antibacterial and anti-inflammatory activity. Distinguished from other heat-clearing herbs by its affinity for the kidney-liver axis.

entities_mentioned.name: 택사 / 澤瀉 (Taeksa / Zexie)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma plantago-aquatica var. orientale (Sam.) Juz. / Alisma orientale (Sam.) Juz. (water plantain rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Promotes urination and drains damp-heat. Key component in the Five-Ingredient Powder with Poria (五苓散 family formulas). Used here to facilitate resolution of fluid-related jaundice.

entities_mentioned.name: 저령 / 豬苓 (Jeolyeong / Zhuling)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyporus umbellatus (Pers.) Fr. — sclerotium (umbrella polypore fungus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Promotes urination, drains dampness. A fungal medicinal; contains ergosterol and polysaccharides. Often paired with Poria cocos for synergistic diuretic effect in damp-heat conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul / Baizhu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (white atractylodes rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies spleen qi, dries dampness. Distinguishable from 蒼朮 (cang zhu / grey atractylodes) by its tonic rather than dispersing quality. Stabilizes the middle burner during treatment for jaundice.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bokryeong / Fuling)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf — sclerotium (tuckahoe / Indian bread / Poria fungus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Calms the mind, tonifies the spleen, promotes urination. A widely used fungal medicinal in East Asian medicine. Works synergistically with Polyporus and Alisma in draining dampness.

entities_mentioned.name: 계지 / 桂枝 (Gyeji / Guizhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J.Presl — young twig (cassia cinnamon twig)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms yang, promotes circulation of qi and blood, assists transformation of water-dampness. Used in small quantity in the Five-Ingredient Powder formulas to warm and move, preventing stagnation of dampness.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃疸 (Hwangdal / Huangdan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jaundice — symptom complex corresponding to icterus; likely encompasses viral hepatitis, biliary obstruction, hemolytic conditions, and alcoholic liver disease in modern terms
entities_mentioned.notes: The Dongui Bogam's classification into five or more subtypes (grain, wine, overexertion, black, yang, yin) represents a clinically sophisticated pre-modern taxonomy. Overlap with conditions such as hepatitis A/B, cirrhosis, cholestasis, and hemolytic anemia is plausible but cannot be mapped one-to-one.

entities_mentioned.name: 黑疸 (Heukdal / Heidan — Black Jaundice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly advanced cirrhosis, hepatic failure, or Addison's disease with jaundice; prognosis noted as grave
entities_mentioned.notes: The darkening of skin and poor prognosis described may correspond to end-stage liver disease with melanosis, or potentially other conditions. Heo Jun's pessimistic prognosis aligns with outcomes in advanced hepatic failure.

entities_mentioned.name: 향약 / 鄕藥 (Hyangyak — 'Local/Native Medicines')
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigenous Korean medicinal plant tradition — not a single plant but a category
entities_mentioned.notes: A defining feature of the Dongui Bogam: Heo Jun systematically documented Korean-sourced equivalents to Chinese materia medica, using Korean vernacular plant names and specifying domestic habitats. This represents a major contribution to the sinification-independence of Korean medicine.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source label is a thematic descriptor rather than a verbatim classical text passage; accordingly, this entry reconstructs the content systematically from the known structure and content of Dongui Bogam Jabbyeong-pyeon Volume 4 (잡병편4) on jaundice, cross-referenced with the herbal agents listed. The entry should be understood as a scholarly synthesis of that chapter's content rather than a direct line-by-line transcription of a single passage.
2. Pun (分, 푼) is a traditional East Asian apothecary weight unit; 1 pun ≈ 0.375 grams in Joseon-era Korean usage, though standardization varied.
3. The Injin-ho-tang formula originates in Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghanlun (傷寒論, c. 220 CE) and Jinkui Yaolue (金匱要略); Heo Jun adapts rather than simply copies it, reflecting Korean clinical experience.
4. Modern pharmacological research has validated several of the hepatoprotective mechanisms implied in this text: capillarisin and scoparone from Artemisia capillaris exhibit choleretic, antifibrotic, and liver-protective activity in vitro and in vivo.
5. The distinction between 白朮 (white atractylodes) and 蒼朮 (grey/blue atractylodes) is clinically important in this tradition; translators should not conflate them.
6. 'Yellow' complexion and urine color as diagnostic signs reflect keen pre-microscopic clinical observation consistent with bilirubin-mediated icterus.
7. The UNESCO Memory of the World designation of the Dongui Bogam (registered 2009) reflects its significance as both a medical and cultural heritage document; translations should preserve its historical context.

results.title_inferred: Treatment of Wasting-Thirst Syndrome (Diabetes) Using Trichosanthes Root, Ophiopogon, Rehmannia, and Schisandra
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam 잡병편5 — 소갈(消渴): 瓜蔞根, 麥門冬, 地黃, 五味子를 주약으로 하는 소갈 치료
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam, Miscellaneous Diseases Section 5 — Diabetes [消渴, So-gal: 'wasting-thirst syndrome']: Treatment of Wasting-Thirst Syndrome Using Principal Medicinals Trichosanthes Root [瓜蔞根, Gwaru-geun], Ophiopogon [麥門冬, Maengmundong], Rehmannia [地黃, Jihwang], and Schisandra [五味子, Omija].

Wasting-thirst syndrome [消渴] is characterized by excessive thirst with copious drinking, frequent and profuse urination, and progressive emaciation [消瘦]. It corresponds broadly to conditions now identified as diabetes mellitus and related metabolic disorders.

In Korean medicine as recorded by Heo Jun, the condition is differentiated into three presentations: Upper Wasting [上消], in which heat damages the Lung [肺] and the patient drinks excessively; Middle Wasting [中消], in which Stomach fire [胃火] consumes food rapidly while the body wastes; and Lower Wasting [下消], in which Kidney yin [腎陰] is depleted and turbid or sweet urine is passed in large amounts.

Therapeutic Approach:
The root pathomechanism is identified as depletion of Yin fluids [陰液虧損] combined with internal dryness-heat [燥熱內盛]. Treatment therefore centers on nourishing Yin [養陰], generating fluids [生津], clearing heat [淸熱], and moistening dryness [潤燥].

Key Medicinals and Their Roles:
1. Trichosanthes Root [瓜蔞根 / 天花粉, Cheonhwabun]: The foremost herb for wasting-thirst in the Korean tradition. Clears Lung and Stomach heat, generates fluids, and relieves thirst. Identified as the root of Trichosanthes kirilowii Maxim. (family Cucurbitaceae). Used dried and powdered or decocted.
2. Ophiopogon [麥門冬, Maengmundong; Ophiopogon japonicus (Thunb.) Ker-Gawl., family Asparagaceae]: Nourishes Lung and Stomach Yin, moistens dryness, clears vacuity-heat, and promotes fluid generation. Particularly indicated for Upper and Middle Wasting.
3. Rehmannia [地黃, Jihwang; Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC., family Orobanchaceae]: Used in both fresh [生地黃] and prepared [熟地黃] forms. Fresh Rehmannia clears heat and cools the Blood, generating fluids; prepared Rehmannia tonifies Kidney Yin and Essence [精]. Especially indicated for Lower Wasting with Kidney deficiency.
4. Schisandra [五味子, Omija; Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill., family Schisandraceae]: Astringes and consolidates Essence and fluids, prevents further loss of Yin, and supplements the Kidneys. Its 'five-flavor' character [五味 — sour, bitter, sweet, acrid, salty] allows it to act on multiple organ systems simultaneously.

Preparation Methods:
These four herbs are most commonly combined in decoction [湯劑, tang-je], wherein the dried medicinals are simmered in water, strained, and the liquid taken warm. They also appear in powdered form [散劑, san-je] mixed with water or honey, and in pill form [丸劑, hwan-je] bound with honey or refined rice paste. Representative formulas from this section include Yukmijihwang-tang [六味地黃湯] and its variants, Maengmundong-eum [麥門冬飮], and Cheonhwabun-san [天花粉散].

Korean Divergence from Chinese TCM:
Heo Jun places particular emphasis on Schisandra [五味子] as an astringent consolidator of fluids — a practice that became more systematized in Korean medicine than in contemporaneous Chinese sources, where Schisandra's role in wasting-thirst formulas was secondary. Additionally, the Dongui Bogam integrates indigenous Korean dietary recommendations alongside herbal treatment, advising restriction of alcohol, fatty foods, and sexual activity [房勞], reflecting the Confucian-influenced moral-medical framework distinctive to Joseon Korea. The text also notes the use of locally sourced Korean Rehmannia [朝鮮地黃], considered by Heo Jun to be of excellent quality and preferentially recommended over imported Chinese stock.

entities_mentioned.notes: Primary herb for wasting-thirst (소갈) in the Korean tradition. The dried root powder (天花粉) clears heat, generates fluids, and relieves thirst. Distinct from the fruit and seed of the same plant.

entities_mentioned.notes: Formula featuring Ophiopogon prominently; indicated for Upper Wasting with Lung-heat and excessive thirst.
