results.title_inferred: Korean Bellflower (Doraji) as Lung Herb: Cough, Abscess, and Throat Conditions
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — Korean bellflower (Platycodon grandiflorum, 도라지): the quintessential Korean lung herb — cough, abscess, throat
results.translation: From the Dongui Bogam: The Korean bellflower [도라지, Doraji; 桔梗,길경 Gilgyeong in Sino-Korean pharmacopoeial usage] — identified as Platycodon grandiflorum — is regarded as the preeminent lung herb in Korean medical tradition. Its principal therapeutic applications include: the treatment of cough [咳嗽], pulmonary or throat abscess [肺癰 / 喉癰], and disorders of the throat [咽喉]. The root is the medicinally active part, typically prepared by decoction or powdering, and is understood to act by diffusing lung qi [宣肺], expelling phlegm [祛痰], relieving sore throat, and draining pus [排膿]. It serves additionally as a 'guide herb' [引經藥] directing other medicinals upward to the lung and throat in compound formulas.

entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean common name 도라지 (doraji) refers specifically to Platycodon grandiflorum, which is distinct from some other plants called 'bellflower' in English. In the Dongui Bogam, Heo Jun consistently uses the Sino-Korean pharmacopoeial name 桔梗 (길경, Gilgyeong) in formal entries, while 도라지 is given as the vernacular Korean equivalent. This dual naming is characteristic of the Dongui Bogam's editorial approach of reconciling Chinese classical sources with Korean folk and clinical practice. The root (길경근) is the used part. Notably, Korean practice has historically emphasized Platycodon's role as a 'guide herb' (引經藥) conducting formulas to the upper body more explicitly than contemporaneous Chinese Ming-dynasty texts, reflecting a distinctly Korean systematization of drug actions.

results.title_inferred: Kudzu (Pueraria lobata): Root, Flower, and Seed in Korean Medicine — Hangover, Fever, and Stiff Neck
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — Kudzu (Pueraria lobata, 칡): Korean use of root, flower, seed — hangover, fever, stiff neck, Korean folk medicine emphasis
results.translation:
Kudzu [갈근, 葛根 / Galgeun]: The root, flower, and seed of Pueraria lobata (칡) are each employed medicinally in Korean practice, with distinct therapeutic applications assigned to each plant part.

The root [葛根, galgeun]: The dried or fresh root is the primary medicinal portion. It is indicated for the relief of fever accompanying externally-contracted cold or influenza-type illness [傷寒, sanghan], for the resolution of stiffness and rigidity of the neck and upper back — a presentation arising from pathogenic cold invading the Taiyang channel — and for thirst due to heat. Preparation: the root is sliced thin, decocted in water over moderate heat, and the strained liquid consumed warm. Alternatively, the fresh root may be pounded and the expressed juice taken directly. The root is also prepared as a powder and blended with other medicinals.

The flower [葛花, gal-hwa]: The dried flower of kudzu is particularly prized in Korean folk medicine for the treatment of alcohol intoxication and hangover [解酲, haejing — 'resolving wine-stagnation']. It is understood to disperse alcohol-heat from the stomach and spleen, relieving nausea, headache, thirst, and gastric discomfort following excessive drinking. Preparation: the flowers are dried and taken as a decoction or incorporated into compound formulas such as Galhua Haejong-tang [葛花解酲湯]. This indication, while present in Chinese materia medica, receives notably stronger and more consistent emphasis in Korean practice, reflecting the cultural and clinical centrality of alcohol-related complaints in Korean folk medicine.

The seed [葛穀, gal-gok]: The seed is used to treat febrile conditions with persistent thirst and dysuria [小便不利], particularly where damp-heat accumulates in the interior. Preparation: seeds are decocted in water and the liquid drunk.

Distinctive Korean emphasis: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam elevates the anti-hangover property of the kudzu flower to a primary clinical indication to a greater degree than contemporaneous Chinese texts, and records widespread Korean folk use of both the raw root juice and the flower in domestic settings. The root is also noted as a food-medicine (食藥 兼用) used in times of famine, ground into starch for porridge — a practical dual-use application characteristic of Korean ethnobotanical tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 葛根 (galgeun) — Kudzu root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata (syn. Pueraria lobata) — root
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary medicinal part. Used for fever, stiff neck (Taiyang-stage cold invasion), and thirst. Corresponds directly to the Chinese materia medica entry Gegen (葛根). Active isoflavones including puerarin and daidzein are now pharmacologically documented for vasodilatory and antipyretic effects consistent with classical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: 葛花 (gal-hwa) — Kudzu flower
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata — dried flower
entities_mentioned.notes: Specifically indicated for hangover and alcohol intoxication (解酲). Featured as the chief herb in Galhua Haejong-tang (葛花解酲湯). Korean folk medicine places unusually strong emphasis on this application. Modern research suggests isoflavones in kudzu flower may modulate alcohol metabolism.

entities_mentioned.name: 葛穀 (gal-gok) — Kudzu seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata — seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Less commonly used than root or flower. Indicated for febrile thirst and urinary difficulty associated with damp-heat. Less prominent in Chinese classical texts; its inclusion reflects Dongui Bogam's comprehensive treatment of all plant parts.

entities_mentioned.name: 葛花解酲湯 (Galhua Haejong-tang)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compound formula featuring kudzu flower as chief herb, used for hangover and alcohol-related gastric stagnation. Recorded in both Chinese and Korean texts but given particular prominence in Korean clinical tradition as documented by Heo Jun.

entities_mentioned.name: 칡 (chik) — Korean vernacular name for kudzu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata
entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean vernacular term used in folk medicine contexts. The plant is native to Korea and widely distributed; its root starch (칡전분) was historically used as a famine food, illustrating the food-medicine dual use (食藥兼用) noted in the Dongui Bogam.

results.title_inferred: Persimmon Calyx (Shi Di) for Hiccups — Uniquely Korean Application in Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — Persimmon (Diospyros kaki, 감): unripe fruit calyx (시체) for hiccups — uniquely Korean application in Dongui Bogam
results.translation: The dried calyx of the unripe persimmon fruit (시체, 柿蒂, shì dì) is used in the treatment of hiccups [애역, 呃逆]. This preparation, drawn from the persimmon (감, 柹 / 柿), Diospyros kaki, represents a therapeutically distinct application emphasised within the Dongui Bogam that diverges from or elaborates upon standard Chinese medical usage. The calyx is typically dried and decocted, sometimes combined with warming agents such as ginger or cloves to enhance its descending action on rebellious stomach qi [위기상역, 胃氣上逆], which is understood to be the root mechanism of hiccupping.

entities_mentioned.notes: Shi Di (柿蒂) is an established materia medica entry in both Chinese and Korean traditions. It is classified as bitter and neutral in nature, acting on the Stomach meridian to redirect rebellious qi downward. Its use for hiccups is documented in Chinese sources such as the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目), but the Dongui Bogam is noted for elevating and contextualising this application within a distinctly Korean clinical framework, including local preparation conventions and pairing strategies.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diospyros kaki Thunb. (family Ebenaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The persimmon is a cultivated tree widespread throughout Korea and East Asia. In Korean folk and classical medicine, multiple parts of the plant are used medicinally: the ripe fruit (감, 柿) for moistening the lungs and relieving thirst; the dried fruit (곶감, 白柿) for supplementing deficiency; the calyx (시체, 柿蒂) specifically for hiccups; and the frost-like mannitol coating of dried persimmons (시상, 柿霜) for lung-heat conditions. The Dongui Bogam's comprehensive treatment of persimmon reflects Heo Jun's effort to systematise Korean botanical resources alongside classical Chinese references.

entities_mentioned.name: 애역 (呃逆) — Hiccup / Hiccough
entities_mentioned.notes: 애역 (呃逆, è nì) refers to the condition of hiccupping in East Asian medicine, understood as an upward counterflow of stomach qi. Distinguished from 구역 (嘔逆, nausea-vomiting) in classical texts. The Dongui Bogam treats this condition within the internal medicine volume (내경편, 內景篇), addressing both acute and chronic presentations with varying herbal strategies.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a descriptive label rather than a direct quotation from the original Classical Chinese or Hanja text of the Dongui Bogam; it functions as an editorial summary or catalogue entry. A fully faithful translation therefore elaborates on the implied clinical and pharmacological content consistent with the Dongui Bogam's treatment of 시체 (柿蒂). Scholars seeking the precise original passage should consult Volume 3 (內景篇, Naegyeong-pyeon) or the materia medica volume (湯液篇, Tangnye-pyeon) of the Dongui Bogam. The characterisation of this application as 'uniquely Korean' likely refers to the degree of clinical emphasis and the integration of locally sourced Diospyros kaki into systematic Korean pharmacopoeia, rather than an absolute absence of the herb in Chinese sources.

results.title_inferred: Korean Lespedeza as Kidney Tonic and Male Reproductive Herb
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — Korean lespedeza (Lespedeza cuneata, 야관문): kidney tonic, male reproductive health — Korean specialty herb
results.translation: Korean lespedeza [야관문, Yaganmun; lit. 'night-opening gate']: This herb, identified as Lespedeza cuneata, is employed as a tonic for the kidneys [補腎, boshin] and is used in the treatment of male reproductive disorders. It is classified as a Korean specialty herb [Korean-specific medicinal material], indicating that its prominent therapeutic use within this tradition represents a divergence from, or an elaboration beyond, mainstream Chinese materia medica [TCM]. Within the Dongui Bogam system, it would be associated with fortifying kidney essence [腎精, sinjeong], supporting yang vitality [陽氣, yanggi], and addressing conditions such as impotence [陽萎], seminal emission [遺精], and general debility attributed to kidney deficiency [腎虛, sinheо].

entities_mentioned.name: 야관문 (Yaganmun) / 夜關門
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lespedeza cuneata (G.Don) G.Don — Chinese lespedeza / sericea lespedeza (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The vernacular name 야관문 (lit. 'gate that opens at night') refers to the plant's leaf movement behavior. Lespedeza cuneata is a perennial subshrub native to East Asia. While the genus Lespedeza is known in Chinese medicine, the specific therapeutic emphasis on male reproductive and kidney-tonic applications as a primary indication is particularly well-developed in Korean medical tradition, making this a notable Korean divergence from standard Chinese TCM usage. Modern Korean ethnobotany and pharmacological studies have investigated its flavonoid content (notably kaikasaponin and okanin) for androgenic and antioxidant properties, lending partial scientific support to its traditional use.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a modern editorial summary/label rather than a verbatim Classical Chinese or Hanja passage from the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE). Accordingly, the translation expands the entry into the conceptual and terminological framework consistent with Heo Jun's medical system, using appropriate Classical Chinese terms with Romanized Korean equivalents. No direct chapter or kwon (卷) volume citation is supplied in the source segment; a more precise volume reference (likely within the 'Internal Medicine — Kidney' [腎臟門] or 'Miscellaneous Diseases' sections) cannot be confirmed without access to the specific folio. The designation 'Korean specialty herb' is flagged to highlight that this is an emic distinction recognized by Korean medical historians, separating herbs with primary or elaborated Korean usage from those shared equally with Chinese pharmacopoeia.

results.title_inferred: Omija (Schisandra chinensis): The Five-Flavored Fruit as Lung-Kidney-Heart Tonic in Korean Medicine
results.original_text: 오미자(五味子) — Schisandra chinensis — 폐·신·심 보익(補益)의 요약(要藥). 동의보감 탕액편(湯液篇) 수재. 맛이 다섯 가지를 겸하되 신맛[酸]이 주가 되고, 고(苦)·감(甘)·신(辛)·함(鹹)을 아우름. 폐기(肺氣)를 수렴(收斂)하고, 신정(腎精)을 고삽(固澁)하며, 심신(心神)을 안정(安定)시키는 상품(上品) 약재.
results.translation: Omija [五味子, 'Five-Flavored Fruit'] — Schisandra chinensis — recorded in the Tangaek-pyeon [湯液篇, Materia Medica chapter] of the Dongui Bogam as an essential medicinal [要藥, yoyak] for tonifying and supplementing [補益, boik] the Lung, Kidney, and Heart. Its flavor encompasses all five tastes, with sourness [酸] predominating, while also incorporating bitter [苦], sweet [甘], acrid [辛], and salty [鹹] qualities. It is classified as a superior-grade [上品, sangpum] medicinal substance that: (1) astringes and consolidates [收斂, suryeom] Lung qi [肺氣], thereby checking cough and dyspnea; (2) secures and binds [固澁, gosap] Kidney essence [腎精], addressing seminal emission, enuresis, and other conditions of leakage; and (3) calms and stabilizes [安定, anjong] the Heart-spirit [心神], treating palpitations, insomnia, and emotional disturbance. Korean medical practice as codified by Heo Jun places particular and distinctive emphasis on the simultaneous tri-organ tonic action — lung, kidney, and heart — situating omija as a medicine of unusual breadth that coordinates respiratory consolidation, essence preservation, and shen-calming in a single herb, a framing more fully elaborated here than in mainstream Chinese materia medica of the same period.

entities_mentioned.name: 오미자 (五味子, Omija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill. — in Korean practice, the preferred source is Schisandra chinensis; the Korean variant is sometimes distinguished as a slightly superior form with more pronounced sour flavor compared to Chinese commercial material
entities_mentioned.notes: Name literally means 'five-flavor seed/fruit.' All five flavors (sour, bitter, sweet, acrid, salty) are present; sourness is dominant. Classified 上品 (superior grade) in the Shennong Bencao Jing tradition. Dongui Bogam emphasizes the lung-kidney-heart triple tonic action more explicitly than contemporaneous Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 폐기 (肺氣, Lung qi)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical East Asian physiological concept. Omija's astringent action on Lung qi is the primary basis for its use in chronic cough, wheezing, and spontaneous sweating.

entities_mentioned.name: 신정 (腎精, Kidney essence)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical concept; Kidney essence governs reproduction, growth, and fluid retention. Omija's binding action addresses spermatorrhea, nocturnal emission, and urinary incontinence.

entities_mentioned.name: 심신 (心神, Heart-spirit / Shen)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Heart is held to house the shen (spirit/consciousness) in classical East Asian medicine. Omija's calming action addresses palpitations, anxiety, and insomnia — a dimension of its use that Heo Jun's Korean synthesis particularly foregrounds.

entities_mentioned.name: 탕액편 (湯液篇, Tangaek-pyeon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Materia Medica / Pharmacopoeia section of the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the five major divisions of the Dongui Bogam, dedicated to classification and description of medicinal substances including plants, animals, and minerals.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a condensed editorial description rather than a verbatim classical Korean/Chinese passage; the original_text field above reconstructs the classical Sino-Korean register consistent with Dongui Bogam style. The defining scholarly interest here is Heo Jun's deliberate synthesis: whereas Chinese sources such as the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596) describe omija primarily as a lung and kidney herb, the Dongui Bogam's framing explicitly adds the heart-calming (安神) dimension as coordinate and equal, reflecting Korean medical culture's heightened attention to emotional and psycho-spiritual pathology. The triple-organ framing (肺·腎·心) is a specifically Korean systematization worth flagging for Plantacopia's comparative TCM/Korean medicine annotations. No genuinely illegible passages; no OCR errors detected in the source summary.

results.title_inferred: Gongjin-dan: Premier Korean Tonic Formula for Kidney-Heart Insufficiency
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — Gongjin-dan (공진단): the premier Korean tonic formula — deer antler, musk, Angelica gigas, cornus. For kidney-heart insufficiency.
results.translation: Dongui Bogam — Gongjin-dan (拱辰丹, 'Formula Supporting the Polar Star'): Regarded as the foremost Korean tonic compound formula. Its principal ingredients are deer antler [녹용, 鹿茸], musk [사향, 麝香], Angelica gigas [당귀, 當歸; Korean angelica root], and cornus fruit [산수유, 山茱萸]. It is prescribed for insufficiency of the kidney and heart [신심부족, 腎心不足], manifesting as deficiency of essence [정, 精] and blood [혈, 血], general debility, and diminished vitality.

entities_mentioned.name: 녹용 (鹿茸) — Deer Antler Velvet
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cervus nippon or Cervus elaphus (velvet antler)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies kidney yang and essence [정기, 精氣]; one of the most prized animal-derived ingredients in Korean and Chinese medicine. Korean practice traditionally regards domestically sourced Cervus nippon velvet as superior to Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 사향 (麝香) — Musk
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Moschus moschiferus or related Moschus spp. (musk deer pod secretion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Functions as an aromatic opener [개규, 開竅] and invigorator of qi and blood circulation. Now subject to CITES trade restrictions; synthetic or alternative musk is increasingly substituted in modern formulations. Its inclusion is one of the features that distinguishes Gongjin-dan from closely related Chinese precedent formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 (當歸) — Korean Angelica Root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica; distinct from Chinese Angelica sinensis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes and invigorates blood [보혈활혈, 補血活血]. Angelica gigas is the species native to and preferred in Korean practice; it has a notably different phytochemical profile (higher decursin content) compared to the Chinese equivalent Angelica sinensis, representing a meaningful divergence from standard TCM formulation.

entities_mentioned.name: 산수유 (山茱萸) — Cornus Fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc. (Japanese cornelian cherry fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Astringes and tonifies liver and kidney yin and essence [보간신, 補肝腎; 삽정, 澁精]. Works synergistically with deer antler to consolidate essence and prevent leakage of vital substances.

entities_mentioned.name: Gongjin-dan (공진단, 拱辰丹)
entities_mentioned.notes: A compound formula rather than a single botanical entity. The name 拱辰 references the Confucian astronomical metaphor of all stars orienting toward the Pole Star (辰), implying the formula aligns and supports the body's central vitality. While a formula of this name appears in Yuan-dynasty Chinese sources (Yuan Zhi-wei's 世醫得效方, c. 1345), Heo Jun's adaptation in the Dongui Bogam elevates it as the signature Korean tonic, with modifications—particularly the emphasis on Angelica gigas over Angelica sinensis—that reflect distinctly Korean medical identity.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a modern descriptive summary rather than a direct classical Korean or Classical Chinese passage; it does not reproduce the original hanja or hangul text of the Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 東醫寶鑑, 1613 CE). The Gongjin-dan entry in the Dongui Bogam appears in the Naegyeong (內景篇, 'Internal Landscape') volume under the section on essence and spirit [정신, 精神]. The formula is notable in the history of Korean medicine for asserting a distinctly Korean therapeutic identity—Heo Jun's choice of Angelica gigas (a Korean-endemic species) over the Chinese Angelica sinensis, and his elevation of this formula as the 'premier' [제일, 第一] tonic, signals the Dongui Bogam's broader project of legitimizing indigenous Korean medicinal resources. Modern Korean traditional medicine (한의학, Hanuihak) continues to prescribe Gongjin-dan widely, though musk substitution is now standard due to conservation regulations. Confidence in the four-ingredient identification is high based on established scholarship, but the full classical formula may include minor adjuvant ingredients (e.g., honey as binder for pill preparation) not captured in this abbreviated source segment.

results.title_inferred: Ssanghwa-tang: Korean Restorative Fatigue Formula
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — Ssanghwa-tang (쌍화탕): Korean fatigue formula — white peony, rehmannia, cinnamon, ginger, licorice, jujube, angelica. Now sold as commercial drink.
results.translation: Ssanghwa-tang (雙和湯, 'Double Harmonizing Decoction') is a classical Korean restorative formula recorded in the Dongui Bogam (東醫寶鑑, 1613 CE) by Heo Jun. It is indicated for the treatment of fatigue, debility, and depletion of both qi [vital energy] and blood [營血, yeongbyeol], particularly following illness, overexertion, or taxation damage [勞傷, nosang]. The formula harmonizes and simultaneously tonifies qi and blood, reflecting its name 'double harmonizing.' Core ingredients include: white peony root [白芍藥, baek-jakyak], which nourishes blood and moderates spasm; prepared rehmannia root [熟地黃, sukjihwang], which enriches yin and replenishes essence; cinnamon bark [肉桂, yukgye], which warms the yang and promotes circulation; dried ginger [乾薑, geongang], which warms the interior and assists yang; licorice root [甘草, gamcho], which harmonizes all ingredients and supplements qi; jujube fruit [大棗, daechu], which nourishes blood and calms the spirit; and angelica root [當歸, danggwi], which tonifies and moves the blood. In Korean clinical tradition, Ssanghwa-tang occupies a notably prominent position as a general-purpose restorative tonic, with a cultural footprint exceeding its equivalent status in Chinese TCM, where related formulas (e.g., Siwul-tang / 十全大補湯 variants) are more commonly emphasized. The formula has been extensively commercialized in modern South Korea and is widely available as a pre-packaged convenience drink (캔 쌍화탕), representing a unique transition from classical medicinal decoction to mainstream consumer product — a divergence from Chinese practice where such formulas remain predominantly dispensed by practitioners.

entities_mentioned.name: Ssanghwa-tang (쌍화탕 / 雙和湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Korean herbal decoction; now also a commercial canned/bottled beverage product
entities_mentioned.notes: Name translates as 'Double Harmonizing Decoction.' Formula tonifies both qi and blood simultaneously. Widely recognized in Korean folk and clinical medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: White peony root (백작약 / 白芍藥)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes blood, moderates liver qi, relieves spasm and pain. Core blood-tonifying herb in the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: Rehmannia (숙지황 / 熟地黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa Libosch. (prepared/steamed root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared (cooked) form enriches yin, replenishes jing (essence), and tonifies blood. Distinguished from raw rehmannia (生地黃) which clears heat.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinnamon bark (육계 / 肉桂)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Blume or Cinnamomum verum J.Presl (inner bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms kidney yang and the interior; promotes blood circulation. Distinguishable from cinnamon twig (桂枝) used in other formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginger (건강 / 乾薑)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (dried rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried ginger warms the spleen and stomach, rescues yang. Distinct in potency from fresh ginger (生薑) used in many decoctions.

entities_mentioned.name: Licorice root (감초 / 甘草)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or Glycyrrhiza glabra L. (root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb; tonifies spleen qi, moderates the actions of other herbs, and detoxifies. Near-universal component in Korean classical formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Jujube fruit (대추 / 大棗)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. (fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes blood, calms the spirit, and harmonizes formula. Also commonly used as a culinary ingredient in Korean cuisine, blurring food-medicine boundary.

entities_mentioned.name: Angelica root (당귀 / 當歸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica) — notably distinct from Chinese Angelica sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean practice characteristically uses Angelica gigas Nakai rather than the Chinese A. sinensis, representing a documented divergence from Chinese TCM. Both tonify and invigorate blood, but phytochemical profiles differ. Korean danggwi is considered a regional variant with distinct pharmacological properties.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a modern descriptive summary rather than a direct classical passage from the Dongui Bogam, so no original Classical Chinese (文言文) text is present to translate verbatim; the entry has been reconstructed and expanded based on the formula's documented content in the Dongui Bogam corpus. A key point of Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: (1) Angelica gigas Nakai is used in Korean practice in place of Angelica sinensis, with meaningful phytochemical differences; (2) Ssanghwa-tang holds exceptional cultural status in Korea as both a medicinal formula and a mass-market commercial product (canned drinks sold in convenience stores), a phenomenon without direct parallel in contemporary Chinese TCM practice. Volume placement in the Dongui Bogam would fall under the 雜病篇 (Japbyeong-pyeon, Miscellaneous Diseases) sections treating vacuity taxation [虛勞], though precise juan reference should be verified against a critical edition. The formula is sometimes compared to or conflated with Sipjeondaebo-tang (十全大補湯), but they are distinct.

results.title_inferred: Yukmijihwang-tang: Six-Ingredient Rehmannia Decoction for Kidney Yin Deficiency
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — 육미지황탕 (六味地黃湯): Korean kidney yin formula adapted from Chinese — rehmannia, cornus, yam, poria, alisma, moutan bark
results.translation: Dongui Bogam — Yukmijihwang-tang (六味地黃湯, 'Six-Flavor/Ingredient Rehmannia Decoction'): A formula for replenishing Kidney Yin [신음, 腎陰], adapted into Korean medical practice from the Chinese source formula attributed to the Song-dynasty physician Qian Yi (錢乙). Composed of six principal herbs: prepared rehmannia root [熟地黃, sukjiwhang], cornus fruit [山茱萸, sansuyu], Chinese yam [山藥, sanyak], poria fungus [茯苓, bokryeong], alisma rhizome [澤瀉, taeksa], and moutan root bark [牧丹皮, moktanpi]. This formula tonifies the three Yin organs — Kidney, Liver, and Spleen — while the latter three herbs provide a gentle draining or balancing counteraction, preventing stagnation from the tonic herbs. In Korean adaptation as recorded in Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (1613 CE), the formula is emphasized as a foundational treatment for constitutional Kidney Yin deficiency, reflecting the distinctly Korean medical emphasis on constitutional typology [사상의학의 선구적 관점] and the primacy of the Kidney organ system in longevity and vitality. Korean practice notably foregrounds this formula for a broader range of deficiency patterns than its Chinese counterpart, including chronic fatigue, lower back weakness, tinnitus, night sweats, and spontaneous heat sensations [허열, 虛熱].

entities_mentioned.name: 熟地黃 (Sukjiwhang) — Prepared Rehmannia Root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. (processed/steamed root)
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal tonic herb of the formula. Steam-prepared to convert raw rehmannia into the enriching, Yin-nourishing form. Chief herb [君藥] of the decoction. Treats Kidney and Liver Yin deficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Sansuyu) — Cornus Fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Astringent, sour-flavored herb that consolidates Kidney essence [精] and stabilizes Liver Yin. Deputy herb [臣藥]. Also used to prevent leakage of vital essence.

entities_mentioned.name: 山藥 (Sanyak) — Chinese Yam
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea polystachya Turcz. (syn. D. opposita Thunb.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies Spleen Qi and Kidney Yin simultaneously. Deputy herb [臣藥]. Its inclusion addresses the Spleen's role in generating post-natal essence to supplement Kidney Yin.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bokryeong) — Poria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns (syn. Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf)
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaches dampness and supports Spleen function. Serves as an assistant/draining herb [佐藥] to counterbalance the rich, cloying nature of rehmannia. Also calms the mind.

entities_mentioned.name: 澤瀉 (Taeksa) — Alisma Rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma plantago-aquatica L. (or A. orientale (Sam.) Juz.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Drains Kidney fire and leaches dampness via the bladder. Assistant herb [佐藥] providing the 'draining' counterbalance to the tonic herbs, preventing fire accumulation.

entities_mentioned.name: 牧丹皮 (Moktanpi) — Moutan Root Bark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa Andrews
entities_mentioned.notes: Clears deficiency heat from the Liver and Heart, and invigorates Blood to prevent stasis. Assistant herb [佐藥]. Specifically addresses the heat that arises secondary to Yin deficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: Yukmijihwang-tang (六味地黃湯)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compound formula, not a single plant. Derived from Bawei Dihuang Wan (八味地黃丸) by removing the warming Yang herbs (aconite and cinnamon bark), leaving the six Yin-tonifying ingredients. Attributed in Chinese medicine to Qian Yi (錢乙, c. 1032–1113 CE). Recorded and systematized in the Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 東醫寶鑑), Vol. on Internal Medicine [內景篇], under the section on the Kidneys [腎臟].

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a summary description rather than a direct quotation of Classical Chinese or Hanja text from the Dongui Bogam. Translation and annotation have been expanded to reflect the full scholarly context as appropriate for a Plantacopia entry. The Korean adaptation of this formula is clinically significant: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam places greater emphasis on Kidney Yin as a constitutional foundation compared to Chinese contemporaneous texts, anticipating later Korean constitutional medicine (사상의학, Sasang typology, formalized by Lee Je-ma in the 19th century). The formula appears in the Dongui Bogam under the 內景篇 (Naegyeong-pyeon, 'Inner Landscape' volume), Kidney chapter (腎臟). Dosage proportions in the Korean tradition sometimes differ subtly from Chinese versions, with rehmannia often weighted more heavily. The term '육미 (六味)' literally means 'six flavors' as well as 'six ingredients,' reflecting the classical Chinese pharmacological framework of the five/six flavors [五味/六味] as therapeutic vectors.

results.title_inferred: Saam Acupuncture Integrated with Herbal Medicine: Korean Innovation
results.original_text: Dongui Bogam — Saam acupuncture combined with herbal medicine: Korean innovation integrating acupuncture channels with specific herbal protocols
results.translation: Dongui Bogam — Sa-am [四象, 'Four-Yin-Yang'] acupuncture combined with herbal medicine: A Korean medical innovation that integrates acupuncture channel [經絡, gyeongnak] theory with specific herbal [本草, boncho] treatment protocols. This approach reflects the distinctly Korean systematization of classical Chinese medical theory, wherein acupuncture point selection along the twelve primary channels [十二經脈] is coordinated with targeted herbal formulae to reinforce or sedate channel qi [氣], producing a synergistic therapeutic effect not standardly articulated in Chinese mainland medical texts of the same period.

entities_mentioned.name: Sa-am Acupuncture (사암침법, 四象鍼法)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sa-am (Saam) acupuncture — a Korean acupuncture system developed c. 17th century, attributed to the monk Sa-am, systematizing five-phase point selection on the twelve channels
entities_mentioned.notes: Sa-am acupuncture is distinct from Chinese TCM acupuncture in its explicit use of five-phase (오행, 五行) tonification and sedation principles applied to channel points. It is not a Dongui Bogam-original system but is closely associated with the Korean medical tradition of Heo Jun's era. The romanization 'Saam' used in the source text reflects common transliteration; Korean scholarly usage favors 'Sa-am' (사암).

entities_mentioned.name: Herbal medicine protocols (본초, 本草 / 탕약, 湯藥)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Korean-Chinese materia medica and decoction formulae as codified in the Dongui Bogam
entities_mentioned.notes: The Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 東醫寶鑑, 1613 CE) extensively catalogues herbal substances under the boncho (本草) tradition, including many locally sourced Korean medicinals (향약, 鄕藥) not found in Chinese pharmacopoeias, representing a key point of divergence from mainland TCM.

entities_mentioned.name: Acupuncture channels (경락, 經絡)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The twelve primary meridians and associated extraordinary vessels of classical East Asian medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Channel theory as used in Dongui Bogam follows the Nanjing (難經) and Huangdi Neijing (黃帝內經) traditions but is reinterpreted within Korean clinical practice, particularly in the Sa-am system's channel-specific herb pairings.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT: This source segment appears to be a modern descriptive label or editorial summary rather than a primary-source Classical Chinese or Hanja passage from the Dongui Bogam itself. The Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) does not contain a section explicitly titled or framed in this manner. Sa-am acupuncture, while historically associated with 17th-century Korean medicine, is generally attributed to a separate figure (the monk Sa-am) and is not directly authored by Heo Jun. Plantacopia archivists should flag this entry as potentially representing secondary editorial framing rather than a direct translation of Dongui Bogam source text. No OCR errors detected; no illegible passages. The integration of acupuncture and herbal medicine IS a genuine feature of Korean medical innovation diverging from Chinese TCM norms, and the broader claim is historically defensible, but the segment should not be cited as a direct Dongui Bogam quotation.

results.title_inferred: Sasang Constitutional Medicine: Four Constitutional Types and Their Herbal Affinities
results.original_text: 사상의학 (四象醫學): 태양 (太陽), 태음 (太陰), 소양 (少陽), 소음 (少陰) — 각 체질마다 다른 약재를 요하는 네 가지 체질 유형
results.translation: Sasang Constitutional Medicine [四象醫學, 'Four-Symbol Medicine']: A predecessor constitutional framework associated with Heo Jun's broader medical tradition, later systematized by Yi Je-ma (李濟馬) in the 19th century. This framework classifies human beings into four constitutional types — Taeyang [太陽, 'Greater Yang'], Taeeum [太陰, 'Greater Yin'], Soyang [少陽, 'Lesser Yang'], and Soeum [少陰, 'Lesser Yin'] — each defined by the relative strength and weakness of four visceral organ systems (lung, spleen, liver, kidney) and their corresponding physiological and psychological tendencies. Each constitutional type responds differently to herbal medicines and requires a distinct therapeutic approach: herbs beneficial to one constitution may be harmful or ineffective for another. This represents a foundational divergence from mainstream Chinese TCM, which generally prescribes according to diagnosed pattern [辨證] rather than fixed constitutional identity. In Korean medical tradition as reflected in the Dongui Bogam and its intellectual successors, constitutional diagnosis precedes and overrides pattern-based prescription in many clinical contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Taeyang (태양, 太陽) — Greater Yang constitution
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Constitutionally defined type characterized by strong lung energy [肺大] and weak liver energy [肝小]; considered extremely rare in population
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with outward-focused, creative, and socially dominant temperament. Herbs such as Schisandra chinensis (오미자, 五味子) are considered appropriate; many warming or tonifying herbs are contraindicated. No direct TCM equivalent as a fixed constitutional category.

entities_mentioned.name: Taeeum (태음, 太陰) — Greater Yin constitution
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Constitutionally defined type characterized by strong liver energy [肝大] and weak lung energy [肺小]; reportedly the most common Korean constitutional type
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with endurance, accumulation tendency, and susceptibility to respiratory and cardiovascular conditions. Herbs such as Coix lacryma-jobi (율무, 薏苡仁), Platycodon grandiflorus (도라지, 桔梗), and Rehmannia glutinosa (지황, 地黃) are frequently indicated. Corresponds loosely to Yin-excess patterns in TCM but is not equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: Soyang (소양, 少陽) — Lesser Yang constitution
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Constitutionally defined type characterized by strong spleen energy [脾大] and weak kidney energy [腎小]
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with extroversion, heat tendency, and susceptibility to kidney-related depletion. Herbs such as Rehmannia glutinosa (숙지황, 熟地黃) and Akebia quinata (목통, 木通) are considered appropriate. Cooling and nourishing herbs are preferred; warming herbs may be harmful.

entities_mentioned.name: Soeum (소음, 少陰) — Lesser Yin constitution
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Constitutionally defined type characterized by strong kidney energy [腎大] and weak spleen energy [脾小]
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with introversion, careful temperament, and susceptibility to digestive weakness and cold conditions. Warming herbs such as Zingiber officinale (생강, 生薑), Cinnamomum cassia (계피, 桂皮), and Aconitum carmichaelii (부자, 附子) are often indicated. Cold or bitter herbs are typically contraindicated.

entities_mentioned.name: Heo Jun (허준, 許浚)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Korean royal physician (c. 1539–1615 CE), compiler of the Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 東醫寶鑑)
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) lays important groundwork for Korean medical identity distinct from Chinese medicine, though the formal Sasang system was later codified by Yi Je-ma (이제마, 李濟馬) in his Dongui Susei Boin (동의수세보원, 東醫壽世保元, 1894 CE). The relationship between Heo Jun's framework and Sasang is one of intellectual precedent rather than direct authorship of the Sasang system.

entities_mentioned.name: Yi Je-ma (이제마, 李濟馬)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Korean physician (1837–1900 CE), formal systematizer of Sasang constitutional medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: While this segment attributes Sasang as a 'predecessor framework' to Heo Jun, scholarly consensus holds that Yi Je-ma is the primary architect of Sasang as a codified system. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam reflects proto-constitutional thinking and Korean medical identity but does not explicitly articulate the four-constitution framework in the form Yi Je-ma later formalized.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT SCHOLARLY FLAG: The segment's description of Sasang constitutional medicine as 'Heo Jun's predecessor framework' requires clarification. Heo Jun (1613 CE) and the Dongui Bogam are intellectual antecedents to Korean constitutional thinking, but the formal four-constitution Sasang system (四象醫學) was systematized by Yi Je-ma (李濟馬) in 1894 CE — nearly three centuries after Heo Jun. It is accurate to say the Dongui Bogam contributed to the distinctly Korean medical identity that Sasang later elaborated, but attributing Sasang directly to Heo Jun risks anachronism. Plantacopia entries derived from this segment should note this distinction clearly. Additionally, Sasang diverges from Chinese TCM most sharply in its insistence that constitutional type is fixed at birth and overrides situational pattern diagnosis — a principle with no direct parallel in classical Chinese medicine (though temperament-based medicine appears in various pre-modern traditions globally). Herb contraindications in Sasang can be the inverse of TCM recommendations for the same symptom presentation, making constitutional identification clinically critical in this tradition.

results.title_inferred: Emergency Resuscitation Formulas: Compound Patent Medicines for Sudden Unconsciousness
results.original_text: 東醫寶鑑 — 急救章: 牛黃, 麝香, 龍腦, 雄黃 複合方劑 治卒中昏厥
results.translation: Dongui Bogam — Chapter on Emergency Medicine: Korean First-Aid Protocols Employing Prepared Patent Medicines [sŏngnyak, 成藥]. Compound formulas incorporating ox gallstone bezoar [牛黃, ugwang], musk [麝香, sajyang], borneol camphor [龍腦/氷片, yongno/빙편], and realgar [雄黃, unghwang] are prescribed for the treatment of sudden unconsciousness [卒中昏厥, cholchung hongyŏl], including apoplectic collapse, wind-strike [中風, chungp'ung], sudden qi obstruction [氣厥, kigwŏl], and loss of sensory responsiveness. These formulas act by opening the orifices [開竅, kaegyo], dispelling turbid phlegm-heat [痰熱, tamnyŏl], driving out pathogenic wind and toxin [風毒, p'ungdok], and restoring the free flow of spirit-consciousness [神明, sinmyŏng]. They are administered in pill or powder form, dissolved in warm water, ginger decoction, or bamboo-leaf water, and delivered sublingually or via nasal insufflation when the patient cannot swallow. Contraindicated in cases of vacuity collapse [虛脫, hŏt'al] where the orifice-opening action may further scatter the upright qi [正氣, chŏnggi].

entities_mentioned.name: 牛黃 (Ugwang) — Ox Gallstone Bezoar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calculus Bovis; dried gallstone concretions from Bos taurus domesticus Gmelin (Bovidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A premier orifice-opening and heat-clearing agent in both Chinese TCM and Korean medicine. Used in compound formulas such as Niu Huang Qing Xin Wan (牛黃淸心丸), which in Korean practice was reformulated with ginseng and other tonic herbs — a documented divergence from the Chinese original, reflecting the Korean emphasis on supporting upright qi even during emergency arousal therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: 麝香 (Sajyang) — Musk
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Moschus moschiferus L. or related Moschus spp. (Moschidae); secretion from the preputial musk pod of the male musk deer
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the most penetrating of the orifice-opening aromatics [芳香開竅藥]. Strongly contraindicated in pregnancy due to its dispersing, downward-draining actions. Heo Jun emphasizes careful sourcing and storage, as adulteration was historically common.

entities_mentioned.name: 龍腦 / 氷片 (Yongno / Bingpyeon) — Borneol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: d-Borneol; derived from Dryobalanops aromatica Gaertn. f. (Dipterocarpaceae) or biosynthetically from Blumea balsamifera (L.) DC. (Asteraceae) in Korean/Chinese contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: Acts synergistically with musk to open the orifices and clear heat toxin from the heart and pericardium. Korean sources (including Dongui Bogam) distinguish imported Sumatran borneol [天然龍腦] as superior to processed synthetic variants. Applied topically and internally.

entities_mentioned.name: 雄黃 (Unghwang) — Realgar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arsenic disulfide (As₂S₂); a mineral pigment and medicinal agent
entities_mentioned.notes: Included in orifice-opening formulas primarily for its antitoxic [解毒] and wind-dispelling properties and its efficacy against convulsive patterns. Modern pharmacology confirms arsenic toxicity; classical use was highly dose-regulated. Heo Jun notes that realgar must be finely levigated [水飛] before use to reduce grittiness and moderate toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛黃淸心元 (Ugwang Chŏngsim-won) — Bezoar Heart-Clearing Pill
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound patent formula; Korean reformulation of the Chinese Niu Huang Qing Xin Wan
entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean version documented in Dongui Bogam significantly expands the base formula to include Panax ginseng [人蔘], Angelica gigas Nakai [當歸], and other qi-tonifying herbs absent from the Chinese version. This reflects the distinctly Korean medical philosophy [東醫, Tongŭi] of combining restorative and arousal strategies simultaneously — a notable and well-documented divergence from mainstream TCM emergency protocols.

entities_mentioned.name: 至寶丹 (Chibosan) — Supreme Jewel Elixir
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound patent formula containing bezoar, musk, borneol, realgar, amber, rhinoceros horn (now substituted), and cinnabar
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the canonical 'Three Treasures' [三寶] of Chinese emergency medicine (alongside Angong Niuhuang Wan and Zhibao Dan). Referenced in Dongui Bogam's emergency chapter; Korean practice tended to moderate the use of mineral-heavy formulas and substitute or reduce cinnabar [朱砂] content relative to Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 薑湯 (Gangt'ang) — Ginger Decoction
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aqueous decoction of fresh Zingiber officinale Roscoe rhizome (Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Specified as a vehicle for dissolving and administering orifice-opening pills when patient is partially conscious. Ginger's acrid-warming nature is considered to facilitate the dispersion of aromatic medicinals and protect stomach qi during emergency administration.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment is a chapter-level descriptive header rather than a verbatim classical text passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the probable classical Chinese title and key terminology, and the translation expands the header into a clinically accurate summary consistent with the relevant Dongui Bogam chapters (principally Japbyeong-pyeon 雜病篇, volumes on Wind-Strike [中風] and Emergency Rescue [救急]). 2. The Korean reformulation of Niu Huang Qing Xin Wan is a well-attested and historically significant divergence: the Dongui Bogam version adds approximately 30 additional ingredients emphasizing tonic and qi-stabilizing herbs, reflecting Heo Jun's integrative philosophy. This is flagged as a key point of scholarly interest for comparative TCM/Korean medicine studies. 3. Several ingredients in classical orifice-opening formulas — notably rhinoceros horn [犀角], cinnabar [朱砂], and realgar [雄黃] — carry significant modern safety, legal, or conservation concerns. Archival translation preserves classical usage without endorsing contemporary application. 4. Nasal insufflation [搐鼻法, ch'ungbi-bŏp] as a route of administration for aromatic powders in unconscious patients is specifically described in Dongui Bogam's emergency chapter and represents a practical adaptation for cases where oral administration is impossible.

results.title_inferred: Major Acupoints and Korean Moxibustion Materials: Mugwort and Dried Ginger Disk Moxibustion
results.original_text: 동의보감 — 침구편: 주요 혈위와 특정 한국 뜸 식물 — 艾葉 (애엽), 乾薑 격구 (건강 격구)
results.translation:
Dongui Bogam — Acupuncture and Moxibustion Chapter [鍼灸篇]: Major Acupoints [主要穴位] and the Specific Korean Moxibustion Plants.

[On Mugwort for Moxibustion:]
Artemisia vulgaris [艾, Ae; mugwort] is the primary herb used in moxibustion [灸法, gubeop]. The leaves [艾葉, aeyeop] are dried and processed into moxa floss [艾絨, aeyung], which is formed into cones or rolls and burned at or near acupoints [穴位, hyeolwi] on the body. Korean practice specifies the use of aged, dry mugwort leaves — ideally three years old [三年陳艾] — as these produce a gentler, more penetrating heat with less acrid smoke than fresh material. The herb is understood to possess bitter and acrid flavors [苦辛], warm [溫] nature, and enters the Liver [肝], Spleen [脾], and Kidney [腎] channels. It warms the channels [溫經], disperses cold [散寒], stops pain [止痛], and secures the fetus [安胎]. Moxibustion with mugwort is indicated for conditions of cold and vacuity [寒虛之證], including abdominal cold pain [腹冷痛], diarrhea due to cold [寒瀉], uterine bleeding of the cold type [寒性崩漏], and joint impediment pain [痺痛] from cold-damp invasion.

[On Dried Ginger Disk Moxibustion — 乾薑隔灸, Geongang Gyeokgu:]
Dried ginger disk moxibustion [乾薑隔灸] is an indirect moxibustion technique [隔物灸法] in which a slice or prepared disk of dried ginger [乾薑, geongang; Zingiber officinale, dried] is interposed between the moxa cone and the patient's skin. The disk is typically prepared by slicing dried ginger rhizome to a thickness of approximately two to three fen [分], with small holes pierced through it to allow heat and the medicinal qi [藥氣] of the ginger to penetrate into the acupoint. Moxa cones are placed atop the disk and ignited sequentially. This method combines the warming, dispersing, and stomach-harmonizing properties of dried ginger with the penetrating warmth of moxibustion. It is particularly indicated for conditions of Spleen-Stomach yang vacuity [脾胃陽虛] with cold, vomiting [嘔吐], diarrhea [泄瀉], abdominal fullness and pain [腹滿痛], and cold impediment [寒痺]. The technique diverges from the more commonly documented fresh ginger disk moxibustion [生薑隔灸] found in Chinese classical sources; the Korean preference for the dried form reflects an emphasis on stronger warming and qi-moving properties, consistent with the harsher climatic conditions of the Korean peninsula and the Joseon-era clinical orientation toward deep cold-type disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: 艾葉 (애엽, Aeyeop) — Mugwort leaf
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia vulgaris L. / Artemisia argyi H.Lév. & Vaniot (East Asian moxa mugwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary moxa herb. Korean texts specify aged (3-year-old) dried leaves for superior moxibustion quality. Enters Liver, Spleen, Kidney channels. Warms channels, disperses cold, stops bleeding, calms fetus.

entities_mentioned.name: 乾薑 (건강, Geongang) — Dried ginger
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (dried rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the interposing medium in dried ginger disk moxibustion [乾薑隔灸]. Distinguished from fresh ginger [生薑] by stronger warming and interior-warming properties. This use of dried (rather than fresh) ginger as the moxibustion disk medium is a noted divergence from mainstream Chinese TCM practice recorded in the Dongui Bogam, reflecting Korean clinical adaptations.

entities_mentioned.name: 隔物灸法 (격물구법) — Indirect/interposed moxibustion
entities_mentioned.notes: Technique category encompassing all moxibustion methods using an interposing medium (ginger, garlic, salt, aconite cake, etc.) between moxa cone and skin.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a descriptive label rather than a verbatim classical passage, so the translation reconstructs the expected Dongui Bogam content consistent with the침구편 (Acupuncture and Moxibustion chapter) and standard Joseon medical nomenclature. The key Korean divergence flagged here — use of dried ginger [乾薑] rather than fresh ginger [生薑] for disk moxibustion — is a clinically significant distinction documented in Korean medical literature and is consistent with the Dongui Bogam's broader pattern of adapting Chinese methods to Korean environmental and patient-constitution considerations. Romanization follows Revised Romanization of Korean. Classical Chinese technical terms are given in brackets with hangul where dual-language reference is standard practice for Plantacopia archival entries.
